questions.retrievalTag: Nocturnal enuresis in children under age 5
questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 3-month history of bed-wetting three to four times weekly. The child's toilet training began at age 2 years; his mother says he required 3 months of training before he was able to stay dry during the day. The patient engages in imaginative play, walks up and down stairs with alternating feet, and enjoys riding his tricycle. He speaks in three-word sentences and his speech is 80% intelligible. He has no history of serious illness. The patient appears cheerful and engages with the physician. Physical examination shows no abnormalities.
questions.educationalObjective: The most appropriate next step in this patient's management is reassurance only, given nocturnal enuresis is considered normal child behavior up until age 5 years.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Normal child development is evidenced by an absence of any red flag signs or symptoms in a child that is meeting their developmental milestones.
Enuresis is normal at age 3 years, and, in the absence of abnormal developmental, historical, or examination features, is not a cause for alarm.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: There is no known prevention for primary enuresis aside from preventative healthcare to promote normal development.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Enuresis related to normal child development will generally resolve with time and reassurance.
Patients with enuresis should undergo a thorough history, physical examination, and relevant laboratory study including urinalysis to rule out medical causes or stressors as the cause.
While many cases of primary enuresis spontaneously resolve, first-line therapies include motivational therapy or enuresis alarms.
Medication therapies, such as desmopressin, imipramine, or oxybutynin, are options if conservative management strategies fail.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient is younger than 5 years, presents in the absence of red flag signs/symptoms, and has been meeting all of his developmental milestones.
If he were older, the most likely diagnosis would be primary enuresis.
Pathologic enuresis would be considered if the patient were not meeting his developmental milestones or had red flag signs/symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choices A, C, and D: Desmopressin therapy, imipramine therapy, and oxybutynin therapy are incorrect. There is no need to treat this patient's nocturnal enuresis, given that it is typical of normal child development and will likely resolve over time. If this patient were aged 5 years or older, it would be best to start with conservative treatment, such as an enuresis alarm or motivational therapy.

Choice B: Enuresis alarm is incorrect. An enuresis alarm would be a reasonable next step if the patient were aged 5 years or older, at which point his presentation would be considered primary enuresis.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman is brought to the office by her husband because of a 3-month history of "bugs" on her skin that neither she nor others have been able to see, but she feels that they make her skin "look ugly." This started following a mission trip to a tropical country in which she received many mosquito bites. She has not had itching, stinging, or swelling. Her husband says she is preoccupied with these bugs; last week, she used rubbing alcohol and bleach on her skin to try to kill them. He says she is otherwise behaving normally, and she continues to go to work, do household chores, and socialize with friends. The patient says she is concerned she will transmit her parasites to others. She has no history of serious medical or psychiatric illness and takes no medications. She drinks one glass of wine nightly. She does not use illicit drugs. Her pulse is 88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg. Examination shows mildly dry skin over the upper extremities but no erythema, excoriations, or other lesions. On mental status examination, she is cooperative but distressed and worried. Her thought process is goal directed. She reports no suicidal thoughts or hallucinations. Results of complete blood count with differential, serum creatinine concentration, and liver and thyroid function tests are all within the reference ranges. A skin scraping shows no evidence of fungal infection.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient most likely has delusional disorder. Delusional parasitosis, as seen in this patient, is a subtype of delusional disorder in which individuals rigidly believe they are infested with parasitic organisms.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Delusional disorder is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions (ie, fixed, false beliefs) for 1 month or longer without other psychotic symptoms (eg, hallucinations).
The disorder does not typically affect an individual's daily functioning.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Delusional parasitosis is a subtype of delusional disorder in which individuals rigidly believe they are infested with parasites, mites, worms, or other organisms.

Delusional parasitosis is typically related to an underlying psychiatric disorder (eg, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder), though some secondary causes include neuropathies, neurologic conditions, such as strokes, stimulant intoxication, infections (eg, leprosy), or adverse effects of medication.
These secondary causes commonly involve sensory disturbances that the patient misinterprets as infestation.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Given the variety of causes, there is no universal strategy for prevention of delusional parasitosis or delusional disorders generally.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Physicians should attempt to build a therapeutic alliance, including acknowledging the patient's distressing symptoms.
Though many patients reject a psychological explanation, antipsychotics should be offered.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Illness anxiety disorder and body dysmorphic disorder both present with beliefs or obsessions related to an individual's body.

However, in illness anxiety disorder, the patient is mostly preoccupied with the possibility that they may have a serious or life-threatening illness (rather than verbalizing a specific belief about infestation) and may experience transient reassurance with negative test results.
Body dysmorphic disorder manifests as preoccupation with a perceived flaw in physical appearance.
Patients with delusional parasitosis are preoccupied by infestation rather than appearance flaws.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Body dysmorphic disorder is incorrect. Body dysmorphic disorder manifests as preoccupation with a perceived flaw in physical appearance leading to repetitive behaviors (eg, checking) or mental acts (eg, comparing appearance to others). Patients with delusional parasitosis are preoccupied by infestation, not appearance.

Choice B: Conversion disorder is incorrect. Conversion disorder, also known as functional neurological symptom disorder, involves neurologic symptoms, such as sensory or motor dysfunction, that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. Patients with functional neurological symptom disorder do not espouse rigid, false beliefs about the cause of their symptoms.

Choice D: Illness anxiety disorder (hypochondriasis) is incorrect. Patients with illness anxiety disorder demonstrate persistently excessive anxiety and preoccupation about the possibility that they may have or may acquire a serious illness, such that they perform frequent health-related behaviors or exhibit maladaptive avoidance. A specific rigid belief about infestation is more consistent with delusional parasitosis.

Choice E: Unspecified delirium is incorrect. Delirium presents with acute disturbances in awareness, attention, and baseline cognition that fluctuate in severity over the course of the day. Other than her infestation beliefs, this patient is cognitively intact.

questions.retrievalTag: Acute mania with psychosis in bipolar disorder
questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is brought to the office by her brother because of a 1-week history of changes in behavior. The brother says she has been talking about her "grand plans" and has spent $5000 on designer clothing during this time. She also has been verbally abusive toward her 15-year-old son. The brother says that she has a 10-year history of weeklong episodes of isolation, during which she eats very little. She has no history of serious medical or psychiatric illness and takes no medications. Their sister was diagnosed with schizophrenia at the age of 25 years. The patient is divorced. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a labile affect and describes her mood as "fine." Her thought process is intermittently coherent with instances of disorganization. She has not had auditory hallucinations, but during the interview she says she needs "a minute of silence" and appears to be listening to something or composing her thoughts.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient is most likely experiencing acute mania with psychosis. She also has a history of depressive episodes. Patients who experience acute mania and depression meet criteria for bipolar disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder characterized by episodes of elevated mood (mania or hypomania) and depression, with significant functional impairment.

This patient is currently in a manic episode, as evidenced by her grandiose thinking and irresponsible spending, with a history of depression evidenced by her weeklong periods of isolation and decreased eating.
In severe cases, patients can experience psychosis, such as this patient's internal stimulation.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Diagnosis of bipolar disorder excludes substance-induced or medical causes.

Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric (and at times labile) mood, increased energy, decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and/or grandiose thinking.
Experiencing acute mania qualifies a patient for a bipolar disorder diagnosis.
Hypomanic episodes feature fewer and less severe manic symptoms compared to acute mania.

Depressive symptoms include depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, psychomotor slowing, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (ie, decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance).

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause of bipolar disorder is unknown, there is no universal way to prevent bipolar disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Mood-stabilizing medication is used to treat acute mood episodes and prevent future mood episodes.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: The differential diagnosis for bipolar disorder commonly includes borderline personality disorder and schizoaffective disorder.
In borderline personality disorder, mood changes typically occur over hours rather than days to weeks.
In schizoaffective disorder, discrete mood episodes may be present, though patients also experience isolated psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B: Brief psychotic episode is incorrect. Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by at least one acute psychotic symptom (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. This patient is experiencing manic symptoms in conjunction with her psychotic symptoms, and has experienced similar episodes in the past, which is more consistent with bipolar disorder.

Choice C: Delusional disorder is incorrect. Delusional disorder is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions (ie, fixed, false beliefs) for 1 month or longer without other psychotic symptoms, such as hallucinations. This patient is experiencing manic symptoms in conjunction with her psychotic symptoms (her internal stimulation indicates she may be experiencing hallucinations).

Choice D: Disruptive mood dysregulation disorder is incorrect. Disruptive mood dysregulation disorder presents with irritability and angry outbursts. The course is typically chronic rather than episodic. Also, this patient demonstrates psychotic symptoms and additional mood symptoms consistent with bipolar disorder.

Choice E: Schizophrenia is incorrect. Schizophrenia disorder involves 6 months or longer of two of the following: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior (known as positive symptoms), or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). The course is typically progressive. Discrete episodes of behavior change are more consistent with bipolar disorder. Also, she demonstrates manic symptoms in conjunction with psychotic symptoms, indicating that her psychotic symptoms are likely secondary to her mood disorder.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of acute abdominal pain for 12 hours. Medical records show a 20-year history of pelvic pain, decreased libido, and multiple abdominal operations. Two months ago, she visited the emergency department because of loss of vision, atypical chest pain, and shortness of breath. Evaluation at that time showed no abnormalities. She appears anxious and irritable and states that her body is "falling apart." Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Examination shows diffuse abdominal tenderness. Laboratory studies and an x-ray of the abdomen show no abnormalities.

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Psychological factors affecting another medical condition

questions.educationalObjective: This patient presents with a history of multiple somatic symptoms, related distress, and healthcare seeking behaviors, consistent with somatic symptom disorder. In somatic symptom disorder, the patient is excessively preoccupied with one or more somatic symptoms such that these symptoms disrupt daily life.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Patients with somatic symptom disorder persistently devote excessive time and energy to symptoms or related health concerns (eg, repeatedly going to the doctor).

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Patients with somatic symptom disorder experience excessive concern about one or more physical symptoms, to the extent that these symptoms interfere with daily functioning.
These symptoms may or may not originate from an underlying illness though are typically unconsciously produced or exacerbated by psychological factors, such as stress, as seen in this patient.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause is unknown, there is no universal prevention strategy for somatic symptom disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Management of somatic symptom disorder centers around regular primary care follow-up that targets coping skills, provides reassurance, and avoids unnecessary tests.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: In factitious disorder, patients consciously exaggerate, produce, or fabricate symptoms.

Though factitious disorder may be on the differential diagnosis for a patient with multiple invasive procedures despite no structural symptom cause, there is no evidence that this patient is falsifying or inducing her symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B: Conversion disorder is incorrect. Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) involves neurologic symptoms, such as sensory or motor dysfunction, that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging.

Choice D: Psychological factors affecting another medical condition is incorrect. Psychological factors affecting another medical condition refers to any mental factor (eg, distress, maladaptive coping, illness denial, health risk behaviors) that inhibits recovery or treatment from a medical condition. This patient's distress is leading her to seek care excessively but is not clearly exacerbating an underlying health condition.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of poor academic performance. He does poorly on written assignments and tests. His teacher told her that he seems to have difficulty understanding written instructions, but his performance on arithmetic tests is good. Physical examination shows no abnormalities.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient demonstrates impairment specific to reading, consistent with developmental reading disorder (also known as dyslexia). Dyslexia involves chronic phonologic processing dysfunction that manifests with reading difficulties and consequent poor academic or occupational performance.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Dyslexia is a specific learning disorder involving dysfunctional phonologic processing that manifests with reading difficulties and consequent poor academic or occupational performance.
These learning difficulties cannot be attributed to global mental conditions, such as intellectual disability, sensory impairment, or other psychiatric or neurologic disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Specific learning disorders are neurodevelopmental conditions associated with impaired function in reading (dyslexia), writing (dysgraphia), or mathematics (dyscalculia).
Diagnosis of dyslexia requires a comprehensive assessment involving standardized testing, cognitive evaluations, and clinical judgment.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: There is no universal prevention strategy, though avoiding risk factors, such as environmental toxins, may help.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Individuals with specific learning disorders benefit from longitudinal assessment and individualized education plans.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Dyslexia is a specific learning disorder caused by cognitive deficits in basic reading skills and should be differentiated from intellectual disabilities and secondary reading problems, such as hearing or vision impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Autism spectrum disorder is incorrect. Autism spectrum disorder is characterized by social communication deficits, repetitive behaviors, restricted interests, and impaired social interaction. This patient does not demonstrate clear social deficits.

Choice C: Social anxiety disorder (social phobia) is incorrect. Social anxiety disorder refers to excessive fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social and/or performance situations. Though social anxiety disorder may inhibit a patient's academic performance, this impairment would be global rather than specific to reading.

Choice D: Specific expressive language disorder is incorrect. Specific expressive language disorder is a communication disorder presenting with difficulties producing spoken language, not in understanding receptive (ie, written) language.
Choice E: Normal development is incorrect. This patient consistently underperforms on academic tasks involving reading, making dyslexia the most likely diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of moderate chest pain. During the past 2 months, he has had increasingly frequent episodes of palpitations, shortness of breath, feelings of impending doom, and fear of losing control. The episodes now occur up to four times weekly. The symptoms resolve with alcohol, so he now drinks alcohol daily. He does not believe that the symptoms are related to alcohol withdrawal. During the past 8 years, he has been in outpatient and inpatient chemical dependency programs five times but has been unable to remain sober for more than 8 weeks after completing the treatment. He also has a 5-year history of anxiety treated with several trials of a variety of antidepressants; he cannot remember the names of those medications and says that they only worsened his symptoms. He currently takes alprazolam intermittently, which he obtains from friends or the emergency department. He says that alprazolam is the only medication that relieves his anxiety, and that he will be able to control his drinking if his anxiety is treated. He is diaphoretic. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8ºF), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 21/min, and blood pressure is 165/97 mm Hg. Physical examination shows tremor; no other abnormalities are noted. On mental status examination, he has an anxious mood and labile affect. Echocardiography and an exercise stress test show no abnormalities. Cardiac enzyme activities are within the reference ranges.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient does not view his alcohol use as a problem, instead rationalizing his drinking behavior as treating his anxiety. This indicates he is in the precontemplation stage of behavioral change.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: The stages of behavioral change are used to define a patient's readiness to change a health-related behavior, such as substance use, diet, or exercise habits.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Patients in the precontemplation stage are not interested in changing their habit and/or may not see their detrimental habit as a problem.
Physicians aim to move patients through the stages of behavioral change over time with an interview technique called motivational interviewing.
Motivational interviewing involves using open-ended, nonjudgmental questions to help the patient explore their reasons for wanting to change or maintain the habit.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Contemplation is the next stage in which patients are ambivalent about changing their habit.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choices B, C, and D: Contemplation, preparation, and action are incorrect. Patients in the contemplation stage may view their behavior as a problem but be unsure about whether they want to change. Patients in the preparation stage have committed to making a change and are ready to discuss strategies and resources to help them make the change. In the action stage, patients start to take steps to change their behavior. This patient does not view his drinking as a problem and is therefore not interested in changing his habit, putting him currently in the precontemplation stage.

Choice E and Choice F: Maintenance and relapse prevention are incorrect. After making the change, the patient must maintain the change and strategize how to cope with temptation to return to the old habit, preventing relapse. This patient has not changed his drinking behavior and is currently not interested in changing his habit.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 24 hours after the onset of agitation and combativeness. She has a history of mental illness treated with haloperidol and benztropine. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. Her skin is dry and erythematous, and her pupils are markedly dilated. The remainder of the neurologic examination is normal. She distractedly gazes off in the corner of the room and does not answer questions except to give the date, which is grossly incorrect.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient's evolving symptoms of dry, flushed skin, hyperthermia, tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, and confusion in the setting of haloperidol and benztropine use are consistent with anticholinergic delirium, which results from blockade of acetylcholine receptors.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Anticholinergic toxicity is a clinical diagnosis and should be suspected in a patient experiencing symptoms such as delirium, relative adrenergic/sympathomimetic excess (eg, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypertension, hyperthermia, tremors, agitation), dry mucous membranes, flushed skin without sweating, visual disturbance, ileus/constipation, and urinary retention, among others, following administration of medications known to block acetylcholine receptors.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Endogenous acetylcholine binding to muscarinic receptors causes smooth muscle contraction of the sweat glands, salivary glands, airways, bladder, and bowel.
Cholinergic neurons projecting to the cerebral cortex mediate attention, learning, and memory.

Antagonism of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors blocks cholinergic signaling in the brain and causes the dysfunction of smooth muscle contraction, leading to cognitive impairment, disorientation, delirium, sedation, mydriasis, anhidrosis (leading to mild hyperthermia and flushed skin in this patient), dry mouth, urinary retention, and/or constipation.
Many medications block acetylcholine receptors, with some common examples including tricyclic antidepressants, antipsychotics, and diphenhydramine.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Knowledge and avoidance of anticholinergic medications is the mainstay of prevention, though prevention may not always be possible.

Patients may require administration of anticholinergic medications for their safety or for appropriate treatment; thus, the benefits of administration may outweigh the risks. Patients receiving anticholinergic medications should be monitored for signs of anticholinergic toxidromes, which should also be treated if they occur.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Treatment of anticholinergic toxidrome includes discontinuing the offending medications and providing pharmacotherapy such as benzodiazepines, which balance the state of relative sympathetic excess in anticholinergic toxicity.
Supportive care with intravenous fluids, antipyretics, and sedatives is also appropriate depending on the severity of symptoms.
In extreme cases, sedation with intubation may be required while the patient metabolizes the medication.

Physostigmine can be used in select cases; however, the appropriate dose to counterbalance the anticholinergic effect may be imprecise or difficult to calculate with accuracy, risking consequent cholinergic excess.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient's sympathetic excess plus signs and symptoms suggestive of acetylcholine deficiency and use of triggering medications narrows the differential diagnosis to anticholinergic delirium.
Differentials such as amphetamine-induced psychosis or neuroleptic malignant syndrome would both present without dry skin, and psychotic symptoms or muscular rigidity would be present, respectively.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Amphetamine-induced psychosis is not the most likely diagnosis. Amphetamines are sympathomimetics, and intoxication is characterized by euphoria, grandiosity, increased wakefulness and attention, agitation, anorexia, and paranoia, which are not demonstrated in this case. On examination, patients can present with signs of sympathomimetic toxicity, including hypertension, tachycardia, tachypnea, diaphoresis, and dilated pupils.

Choice C: Hallucinogen intoxication delirium is not the most likely diagnosis. In cases of hallucinogen intoxication delirium (such as with cannabis or LSD use), patients typically present with hallucinations as a prominent presenting symptom without signs of anticholinergic toxidrome, such as hyperthermia, dry, flushed skin, or cycloplegia.

Choice D: Major depressive disorder is incorrect. Major depressive disorder presents with depressive symptoms, anhedonia, guilt, low energy, concentration changes, appetite changes, sleep disturbances, and psychomotor slowing, and may demonstrate psychotic or suicidal features. Autonomic instability, such as hyperthermia, tachycardia, and hypertension, would be atypical.

Choice E: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is incorrect. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome presents with muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, tachycardia, and hypertension in the setting of antipsychotic use. This patient has a normal neurologic examination, which would not be the case in neuroleptic malignant syndrome, as an abnormal motor examination due to rigidity is a defining feature.

Choice F: Schizophrenia, paranoid type, is incorrect. Patients with schizophrenia, paranoid type, present with paranoia, delusions, disorganized speech, and disorganized thinking, along with negative symptoms, such as poor hygiene. This patient lacks characteristic features of an exacerbation of schizophrenia and has autonomic instability, which would not occur as a primary feature of schizophrenia.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother for an annual well-child examination. The patient expresses no concerns but his mother says that he has a 3-month history of fatigue and that she has had difficulty waking him in the morning. He also has frequent headaches that are not relieved by acetaminophen or ibuprofen. His mother initially thought he was exhibiting "normal teenage behavior," but she is now concerned because his grades have fallen from A's to C's. The patient's teachers note that he always seems tired during school. He falls asleep without difficulty and sleeps 9 hours nightly but still feels tired upon awakening. His appetite is normal. He has no significant medical history and takes no medications. He is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 64 kg (140 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows large tonsils and adenoids; the remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he describes his mood as "kind of down" and his affect is congruent. He has not had suicidal or homicidal ideation.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient, with headaches, fatigue, trouble waking, and enlarged tonsils, likely has obstructive sleep apnea hypopnea (OSAH). The most common cause of pediatric OSAH is tonsillar and adenoid hypertrophy.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: OSAH is common in the pediatric population and often presents with excessive daytime sleepiness, headaches, and snoring.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: In obstructive sleep apnea, transient collapsing of the upper airways during sleep results in hypopnea (partial obstruction) or apnea (complete obstruction). Obstructive sleep apnea in adults is most commonly caused by pharyngeal crowding from obesity, while in pediatric patients, it is most commonly caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy, particularly in children with a normal BMI.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: While obstructive sleep apnea from obesity can be prevented by maintaining a normal BMI, there is no prevention strategy for OSAH caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: The first-line treatment of pediatric OSAH caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy is adenoidectomy and tonsillectomy. In some cases, watchful waiting is appropriate if symptoms are mild.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient presents with fatigue, headaches, and large tonsils/adenoids, making the most likely diagnosis OSAH.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Hypothyroidism is incorrect. Hypothyroidism can cause fatigue and depressed mood, cold intolerance, and weight gain. Persistent headaches and large tonsils/adenoids are symptoms more consistent with OSAH, not hypothyroidism.

Choice B: Major depressive disorder is incorrect. The patient does report feeling down, but this is secondary to the fatigue and symptoms he is experiencing from OSAH. While major depressive disorder can be diagnosed in the presence of another medical illness, the patient does not meet criteria.

Choice C: Narcolepsy is incorrect. While daytime sleepiness can be seen with narcolepsy, narcolepsy tends to occur suddenly and is associated with cataplexy and hallucinations either before or after sleep. Chronic headaches are not seen in narcolepsy.

Choice E: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) is incorrect. A persistent depressive disorder diagnosis requires at least 2 years of depressed mood with symptom-free periods not exceeding 2 months. Depressed mood in this patient is most likely secondary to the fatigue and symptoms he is experiencing from OSAH.

Choice F: Normal stage of adolescent development is incorrect. While adolescents can present with changes to sleep patterns, such patients should only be symptomatic if the changes lead to inadequate sleep (eg, going to bed at 1 am and waking up at 6 am to go to school). The patient is getting sufficient sleep, and his symptoms are best explained by OSAH.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman is brought to the office because of a 1-week history of episodes of dizziness, nausea, headache, and paresthesias. Her symptoms are exacerbated when she moves her head, especially when she turns it rapidly. She began taking paroxetine for generalized anxiety disorder 3 months ago but discontinued it abruptly 2 weeks ago because she did not feel it was improving her symptoms. Pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 150/78 mm Hg. Physical examination shows increased muscle tone and a mild resting tremor of the hands. Mental status examination shows a tense, anxious affect. She speaks at a normal rate with normal response latency. Her thought process is logical. Hemoglobin concentration is 13 g/dL, serum sodium concentration is 134 mEq/L, and serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration is 2 uU/mL.

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH (vasopressin)

questions.educationalObjective: The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) withdrawal. Abruptly discontinuing antidepressants with short half-lives, such as paroxetine, can lead to SSRI withdrawal, which manifests as the acute onset of flu-like symptoms, neurologic symptoms, and psychiatric symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: SSRI withdrawal (also known as antidepressant discontinuation syndrome) manifests as the acute onset of flu-like symptoms, neurologic symptoms (including "brain zaps" or electric shock-like symptoms of the head, headache, and paresthesias), and psychiatric symptoms (including irritability, anxiety, and insomnia).
Tremors can occasionally occur as well.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Symptoms typically occur within days of abruptly discontinuing an antidepressant that has been taken chronically.

Discontinuation of antidepressants with short half-lives, including venlafaxine (a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor) and paroxetine (an SSRI), are most likely to lead to substance withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: SSRI withdrawal can be prevented by slowly tapering down the use of antidepressant medications.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Most cases of SSRI withdrawal are mild and self-limited, requiring supportive care only.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Physicians should exclude a worsening of the patient's underlying anxiety disorder.
However, anxiety in conjunction with flu-like and neurologic symptoms is most consistent with SSRI withdrawal.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Benign positional vertigo is incorrect. In benign positional vertigo, patients present with minutes-long, abrupt episodes of vertigo that may be accompanied by nausea, which resolve when holding still. An acute course days after paroxetine discontinuation and associated anxiety are more consistent with SSRI withdrawal.

Choice B: Functional neurologic symptom disorder is incorrect. Functional neurologic symptom disorder (conversion disorder) involves acute neurologic symptoms, such as sensory or motor dysfunction, that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. This patient's symptoms and physical examination findings are instead consistent with SSRI withdrawal.

Choice C: Illness anxiety disorder is incorrect. Illness anxiety disorder presents with persistently excessive anxiety and preoccupation about the possibility that they may have or acquire a serious illness such that they perform frequent health-related behaviors or exhibit maladaptive avoidance. Though this patient appears anxious, she does not report a clear psychological cause of her anxiety and does not demonstrate maladaptive healthcare-seeking behaviors.

Choice D: Multiple sclerosis is incorrect. Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. This patient's neurologic symptoms do not localize to a particular central nervous system region and are not separated in time.

Choice F: Syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH (vasopressin) is incorrect. Though it can be an adverse effect of antidepressant medication, syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH is asymptomatic unless hyponatremia is severe (unlike this patient's mild hyponatremia), which may present with weakness, headache, seizures, and coma.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man comes to the office because of a 4-week history of erectile dysfunction. The patient was married for 20 years, but his wife divorced him 2 years ago because of his excessive drinking. He reports that since the divorce, he could only obtain occasional erections during masturbation. The patient reports that since resuming relationships with women his ability to maintain an erection has been intermittent, but now that he is interested in a long-term relationship with his current partner he is concerned that she might leave him if his sexual performance does not improve. He says that he blames himself for his divorce and feels guilty for the way he treated his wife. The patient has a 20-year history of alcohol use disorder and major depressive disorder; he also has gastroesophageal reflux disease. Medications are atorvastatin and fluoxetine, which he has been taking for 10 years. He drinks six beers daily. Vital signs are within normal limits, and physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges.
questions.educationalObjective: Alcohol use is a common cause of erectile dysfunction, affecting the nerves and blood supply to the penis. Continued significant alcohol use and the presence of symptoms during masturbation make alcohol use the most likely cause of his erectile dysfunction.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Difficulty achieving or maintaining an erection during sexual intercourse and masturbation in a male with heavy alcohol use is most consistent with alcohol-induced erectile dysfunction.
Alcohol-induced erectile dysfunction can cause distress and interpersonal difficulties within relationships.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Alcohol is a depressant that can damage the neurovascular supply of the penis.
Both chronic and acute significant alcohol use can result in erectile dysfunction.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: The best prevention for alcohol induced erectile dysfunction is abstinence from alcohol.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Abstinence or decrease in alcohol use can result in improvements in alcohol-induced erectile dysfunction.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient presents with a 4-week history of erectile dysfunction with sexual intercourse in the setting of chronic significant alcohol use.

Although his guilt and anxiety from his sexual performance seems to be significant, he has experienced intermittent erectile dysfunction with masturbation, which makes psychological factors a less likely primary contributor.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B and Choice E: Anxiety about his sexual performance and guilt are incorrect. Psychological factors are a common cause of erectile dysfunction. However, psychological factors only cause erectile dysfunction during sexual intercourse and tend to fluctuate with the mental health of the individual and the stability of the relationship. Psychological factors would not cause erectile dysfunction with masturbation. Alcohol use is more likely in an individual with a history of alcohol use disorder who drinks six beers daily.
Choice C: Atorvastatin is incorrect. Erectile dysfunction is not a known adverse effect of atorvastatin.

Choice D: Fluoxetine is not the most likely cause. Fluoxetine can cause delayed ejaculation and erectile dysfunction. However, this patient has been taking fluoxetine for 10 years, and it would be unlikely for this adverse effect to suddenly emerge. In a patient who drinks significant amounts of alcohol, alcohol-induced erectile dysfunction is a more likely diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man is evaluated 1 week after admission to the hospital because of burns over his back and upper extremities that cover over 20% of his total body surface. He sustained the burns during an accident in his kitchen while he was intoxicated. During his hospitalization, he has had numerous debridement procedures and receives multiple antibiotics for infection prophylaxis. The patient wants to leave the hospital and is evaluated to determine if he has the decision-making capacity to leave. He says he wants to return home because he is tired of being in the hospital. He knows he has suffered a serious burn and has undergone extensive treatment. He reports he has had a burn before on his left lower extremity and points to a 3 x 4-cm area. The patient describes how he cared for that burn himself, and it improved on its own. For his current burn, he says he can get a nurse to give him antibiotics at his home. He believes his physicians are exaggerating the risk related to going home and are wrong about him needing more surgical procedures. The physician confirms the patient lacks the capacity to leave the hospital against medical advice.

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Inability to appreciate the situation and its consequences

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Lack of understanding of relevant information

questions.educationalObjective: This patient repeatedly demonstrates an inability to appreciate the extent of his burns and his ability to manage them himself in the home environment. A lack of capacity to refuse treatment can be decided if the patient is unable to clearly articulate an accurate understanding of their medical situation and the likely result of their preferred management.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: A patient with an inability to appreciate the situation and its consequences does not have freedom of decision.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: A patient may lack the capacity to refuse treatment if they cannot explain their medical condition and what would happen if their management strategy were to be pursued.

A patient without capacity will present with a fundamental misunderstanding of their medical situation (eg, falsely equating his current serious burn to a minor prior burn as seen in this patient) and demonstrate expectations that are incongruent with reality (eg, expecting to get a nurse at home on his own).
There may be a clear reason for this cognitive deficit (eg, a stroke) or not.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: The patient's misunderstanding of how serious this burn is, and the belief he does not need more surgery and can get his own nurse to take care of him at home, points to an inability to appreciate the situation and its consequences.
He clearly communicates a choice, and while incorrect in his assertions, he does not attempt to fabricate information.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Failure to communicate a choice is incorrect. This patient clearly states that he wants to leave the hospital.

Choice C: Lack of understanding of relevant information is not the most likely indication. This patient understands that he has suffered a serious burn, has undergone extensive treatment, and requires additional treatment. It is his inability to appreciate the severity of this situation and its consequences that most clearly indicates his lack of capacity to refuse treatment.

Choice D: Poor manipulation of information is not the most likely indication. Poor manipulation of information implies the patient lacks the cognitive ability to receive and interpret information provided by an outside source (eg, for this patient, his care team). He seems to have fundamental misunderstandings of the significance of his condition and does not appreciate the risk; however, he seems to be able to receive and interpret information without issue.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old medical resident comes to the office because of difficulty speaking in front of his attendings and professors. In these situations, his voice quivers, and he becomes flushed, diaphoretic, and tremulous. He does not experience these symptoms when he is home alone or with family, friends, or patients. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he says that he is self-conscious and gets nervous when he has to talk in front of people who are judging his ability to become a good physician.
questions.educationalObjective: Some patients with social anxiety disorder experience anxiety that is specific to performance situations only. Social anxiety disorder refers to an excessive fear of scrutiny, embarrassment, or rejection in social or performance situations leading to significant distress and/or impaired functioning, as in this patient's case when speaking in front of his attendings and professors.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Social anxiety disorder is characterized by an intense fear of being judged, embarrassed, or rejected in social or performance settings, resulting in significant distress or functional impairment.
Patients with social anxiety disorder commonly exhibit shyness and performance anxiety.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Performance anxiety refers to the fear of negative evaluation in social and performance situations, such as public speaking, test-taking, and sexual activity.
Some patients with social anxiety disorder experience anxiety that is specific to performance situations only, such as this patient's lack of symptoms with family, friends, or patients.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause is unknown, there is no universal strategy to prevent social anxiety disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: In the performance-only subtype of social anxiety, cognitive behavioral therapy is recommended as the first-line treatment, though as-needed anxiety medications (eg, benzodiazepines, propranolol) are frequently used as well.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Panic attacks typically involve somatic symptoms of anxiety (eg, diaphoresis, dyspnea, paresthesia) and may occur in a variety of anxiety disorders (most commonly panic disorder).
Though this patient experiences somatic anxiety symptoms, these symptoms do not meet criteria for a panic attack.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Adjustment disorder with anxiety is incorrect. Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor. Patients with adjustment disorder may experience associated depression or anxiety symptoms but do not meet the diagnostic criteria for depression or anxiety disorders. This patient meets criteria for social anxiety disorder.

Choice B: Generalized anxiety disorder is incorrect. Generalized anxiety disorder is an anxiety disorder that features excessive worrying about a range of topics with associated functional impairment for longer than 6 months. This patient's anxiety is specific to social performance situations.

Choice C: Panic disorder is incorrect. Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. This patient's symptoms do not clearly meet full panic attack criteria. As well, his anxiety is expected (ie, triggered by social performance).

Choice E: Specific phobia is incorrect. Specific phobia is a psychiatric disorder featuring anxiety about a certain situation or object that leads to avoidance behavior. This patient's performance anxiety is best characterized by the more specific diagnosis of social anxiety disorder.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-month history of difficulty achieving orgasm. She has been sexually active with one male partner during the past 2 months; they use condoms consistently. Prior to her current partner, she had not had sexual intercourse since getting divorced 4 years ago. The patient was diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder 3 months ago; citalopram therapy was initiated at that time. Her menstrual cycles have become irregular during the past 6 months and occur at 4- to 6-week intervals. She also has a history of hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease for which she takes amlodipine and omeprazole. Vital signs are within normal limits, and physical examination discloses no abnormalities.

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Normal response to new relationship status

questions.educationalObjective: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), such as citalopram, are a common cause of anorgasmia. Citalopram was initiated 1 month prior to onset of symptoms, making it the most likely cause.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Anorgasmia, or the inability or failure to experience orgasm, is common in women (occurring in about 25% of sexually active women) and frequently multifactorial.
Recently initiated SSRIs are a known cause of anorgasmia in the absence of another clear cause.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Anorgasmia can be idiopathic, related to relationship problems, stressors or anxiety, or medications, among other causes.
SSRIs are a common cause of anorgasmia.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: While there is no specific way to prevent anorgasmia, choosing medications that are less likely to precipitate anorgasmia when feasible may prevent iatrogenic causes.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Anorgasmia due to a medication can be treated by discontinuing the medication.
If medication management of a mood disorder is still required, initiation of medications that are not known to cause sexual dysfunction may be warranted (eg, bupropion).

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient presents with a 2-month history of anorgasmia after starting citalopram 3 months ago.
There is no evidence of competing causes of anorgasmia. Amlodipine, omeprazole, and perimenopause do not cause anorgasmia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Amlodipine is incorrect. Amlodipine is not known to cause anorgasmia in women. Side effects of amlodipine include peripheral edema and orthostatic hypotension.
Choice C: Omeprazole is not the most likely cause. Although there are case reports of omeprazole being associated with anorgasmia, SSRI-induced anorgasmia is common and well-studied.

Choice D: Perimenopause is incorrect. Menopause can cause vaginal dryness, which could cause difficulty achieving orgasm. However, the patient is perimenopausal (as evidenced by her irregular menstrual cycles) and reports no vaginal dryness or other sexual problems.

Choice E: Normal response to new relationship status is incorrect. While anorgasmia can occur because of relationship problems, this patient does not report any problems. Anorgasmia is not considered a normal response to entering a new relationship, unless coupled with anxiety, for which this patient is currently treated.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old primigravid woman at 15 weeks' gestation comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of progressive anxiety and numbness and tingling in her tongue and breasts. She has switched from full-time to part-time status in her work as an accountant "so that I won't stress the baby" but says she feels more overworked than ever. She says it is time consuming to figure out how many diapers she will need, what the best schedule for pumping will be, and how to transform her home to a "safe house for a baby." She has a history of migraines, psoriasis, and major depressive disorder. Her medications are bupropion, sumatriptan, and a prenatal vitamin. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 15-week gestation. On mental status examination, she has a euthymic mood and anxious affect. Her demeanor is earnest, and she is fastidious in her descriptions. She has a furrowed brow. Cognition is intact.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient most likely has perinatal generalized anxiety disorder, which commonly involves excessive and persistent worrying about fetal, birthing parent, or partner well-being. The most appropriate next step in management is treatment with cognitive behavioral therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Perinatal generalized anxiety disorder commonly involves excessive and persistent worrying about fetal, birthing parent, or partner well-being leading to the disruption of daily functioning (eg, excessive behaviors related to anxiety, work dysfunction).
Anxiety can also lead to paresthesia in several body areas, especially in the perioral region and extremities via several mechanisms (eg, muscle tension, attentional bias, etc.).

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Though pregnant patients may not meet the 6-month duration criteria for generalized anxiety disorder, some researchers have advocated for a perinatal generalized anxiety disorder diagnosis with a decreased duration criterion of 1 month.
Patients with premorbid psychiatric disorders are vulnerable to peripartum mood and anxiety disorders, such as perinatal generalized anxiety disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Counseling interventions may help prevent peripartum mood and anxiety disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Cognitive behavioral therapy is first-line treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: In severe anxiety disorders, antidepressant or anxiolytic medications are commonly added.
However, nonpharmacological interventions are preferred in pregnant patients to decrease fetal medication exposure.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Buspirone therapy is incorrect. Buspirone stimulates serotonin receptors and may be useful as a second-line treatment in generalized anxiety disorder. However, cognitive behavioral therapy is considered first-line therapy, particularly in pregnant patients.

Choice B: Clonazepam therapy is incorrect. Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine that is useful in refractory anxiety disorders. However, cognitive behavioral therapy is considered first-line therapy, particularly in pregnant patients.
Choice D: Neurologic evaluation is incorrect. This patient's numbness and paresthesia cannot be localized to a specific central nervous system region and are likely resulting from her anxiety.
Choice E: Reexamination in 1 month is incorrect. This patient is experiencing impairing anxiety and requires immediate treatment.

questions.retrievalTag: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood criteria
questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman found evidence suggesting that her husband had an affair 1 month ago. Despite his denials over the past month, she has crying spells, loss of appetite, no interest in marital relations, and difficulty concentrating when reading or watching television. She states that she felt this way once before when she was in college and broke up with a boyfriend.

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Major depressive episode with atypical features

questions.educationalObjective: This patient presents with a handful of depressive symptoms (eg, appetite loss, concentration impairment, episodic decreased mood) after a stressor, which is consistent with adjustment disorder with depressed mood. Adjustment disorder presents with anxiety, depressive, and/or behavioral symptoms that do not fully meet anxiety or depressive disorder criteria.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Adjustment disorders involve emotional or behavioral symptoms, such as anxiety or depression, that arise in response to a stressor but do not meet full criteria for an anxiety or depressive disorder.
This patient's behavior over the past month after a significant relationship stressor is most consistent with adjustment disorder with depressed mood.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of an emotional disturbance within 3 months of an identifiable stressor (eg, significant relationship stressors).
The emotional disturbance manifests as marked distress out of proportion to the stressor and a significant impairment in daily functioning.
Symptoms do not meet the criteria for other disorders and typically resolve within 6 months of the stressor ending or patient adaptation to the stressor.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Environmental modification to remove the stressor, when possible, can prevent adjustment disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: As adjustment disorder is typically self-limited, the treatment of adjustment disorder commonly includes brief supportive psychotherapy, though brief pharmacotherapy that targets associated symptoms may be empirically provided.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Adjustment disorder should be differentiated from major depressive disorder, which can present with five or more depressive symptoms after a stressor.
By definition, adjustment disorder with depressed mood presents with fewer than five depressive symptoms (eg, low mood, tearfulness, or feelings of hopelessness).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B: Brief psychotic disorder is incorrect. Brief psychotic disorder is characterized by at least one acute psychotic symptom (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior) lasting less than 1 month. This patient does not demonstrate psychotic symptoms.

Choice C: Cyclothymic disorder is incorrect. Cyclothymic disorder refers to alternating hypomanic (eg, euphoric or irritable mood, increased activity level, decreased need for sleep) and depressive symptoms that do not meet full criteria for major depressive or manic episodes. This patient does not possess a history significant for manic symptoms.

Choice D: Major depressive episode with atypical features is incorrect. Major depressive disorder involves 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (ie, decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Major depressive disorder with atypical features involves hypersomnia, hyperphagia, mood reactivity, rejection sensitivity, and leaden paralysis. This patient does not meet the symptom number criteria for major depressive disorder.

Choice E: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) is incorrect. Persistent depressive disorder refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least 2 years. This patient does not meet duration criteria for persistent depressive disorder.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because she is concerned about his recent behavior. She says that he frequently stays out late with friends and has not been forthcoming about his activities. She also believes that he drinks alcohol with his friends. He no longer wants to participate in family game night. He maintains a B+ average in school and has an after-school job. When interviewed alone, he rolls his eyes and says that he is embarrassed by his mother's concerns. He states that he and his friends occasionally drink beer on the weekends, but most of the time they play video games and talk. He has tried cannabis twice. He says that he has not had any recent change in mood or sleep. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormalities.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient, a teenager maintaining relationships with friends and family, and with consistent academic performance, is demonstrating normal development. Late adolescence often involves increased independence, boundary testing, staying out late, prioritizing peers over family, and experimenting with substances.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Individuals in their late teenage years will normally seek independence and may try alcohol, tobacco, or other substances.

In the absence of problems with functioning at school and/or work, symptoms of a new mood disorder, engaging in violence or other illegal activity, or evidence of substance use disorder, this patient's presentation is typical of normal development.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Shifts in the behavior of teenagers may occur over time.
Normal adolescent behavior involves seeking independence and forming identity, often through boundary testing and social exploration.
Common changes include spending more time with peers, arguing more, staying out later, and experimenting with substances.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: There is no prevention for a normally developing teenager.

Prevention of substance use disorder may include treating psychiatric comorbidities, fostering strong social and community connections, and employing evidence-based interventions for preventing substance use disorders.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: There is no treatment for normal adolescent development. Treatment for substance use disorder may include inpatient or outpatient substance treatment programs, cognitive behavioral or other psychotherapy, or medication assisted treatment as appropriate.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: The patient is staying out late, predominantly spending time with his friends, and experimenting with drug and alcohol use, which are all features of a normally developing teenager.
Substance use disorder should be considered but is less likely in the absence of decline in his functioning.
The patient has no symptoms of a mood disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Alcohol use disorder is incorrect. There is no evidence in this patient of a persistent pattern of alcohol use or clear problems related to the use of alcohol. Alcohol use must be impairing the patient's functioning to qualify as a disorder.

Choice B: Cannabis use disorder is incorrect. The few times this patient has tried cannabis with no clear adverse effects does not meet the criteria for cannabis use disorder. Cannabis use must interfere with functioning in life to qualify as a disorder.

Choice C: Oppositional defiant disorder is incorrect. Oppositional defiant disorder manifests as a persistent pattern of angry mood, defiant behavior, and vindictiveness that causes distress and impaired functioning in the child or their caretakers. This patient's boundary testing behavior is not borne out of the need to rebel against authority but is typical of normal adolescent development.

Choice D: Parent-child relational problem is not the most likely explanation for this patient's findings. This patient's behavior is not secondary to a poor relationship with his parents and is typical of normal adolescent development.

Choice E: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) is incorrect. Persistent depressive disorder requires at least 2 years of depressed mood without any periods greater than 2 months of symptom resolution. There is no evidence of a persistently low mood in this patient, and his behaviors are normal for his age.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man is brought to the office by his parents because of changes in his behavior during the past 6 months. During this time, he has been obsessed by numbers and equations and spends hours in his room thinking and calculating. His parents say that he has always been serious, curious, and quiet. The few friends he had stopped interacting with him because of his behavior. The patient explains that he needs to solve "the ultimate and consummate algorithmic result." He has no history of serious illness. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 98 kg (217 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m2. Pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 116/77 mm Hg. Physical examination shows abdominal striae. On mental status examination, he describes his mood as "transfixed." He has a blunt affect. His thought process is disorganized, and he continues to talk about the equation. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Urine toxicology screening is negative. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient most likely has schizophrenia. Schizophrenia is characterized by at least 6 months exhibiting two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions (eg, this patient's fixation on solving an equation), hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat or blunted affect, apathy, alogia). Delusions may center around pre-existing themes in a patient's life. Many patients also illustrate prodromal periods of strange behavior and decreased functioning. The total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months to meet diagnostic criteria.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: The cause of schizophrenia is thought to include dopamine dysregulation, and brain imaging typically shows decreased cortical volume and enlarged ventricles. A family history of bipolar disorder or schizophrenia is common in patients who are diagnosed.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Though there is no universal way to prevent schizophrenia, avoiding cannabis use during adolescence (especially in patients with a family history of serious mental illness) is likely beneficial for decreasing future schizophrenia risk.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Treatment of schizophrenia requires antipsychotics, which can be divided into two groups: typical (eg, haloperidol, fluphenazine, chlorpromazine) and atypical (eg, quetiapine, aripiprazole, olanzapine).

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: In obsessive-compulsive disorder, patients experience recurrent, intrusive, distressing thoughts (ie, obsessions) and perform repetitive mental or physical actions (ie, compulsions) to relieve the anxiety related to their obsessions. However, schizophrenic delusions are typically more rigid, and patient affect is more likely to be anxious than blunted as negative symptoms are uncommon in obsessive-compulsive disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Avoidant personality disorder is incorrect. Avoidant personality disorder is a cluster C (anxious or fearful) personality disorder that features the avoidance of interpersonal contact due to feelings of social inadequacy and hypersensitivity to rejection. This patient's social avoidance is related to delusions rather than fear of rejection.

Choice B: Bipolar disorder is incorrect. Bipolar disorder consists of episodes of mania, frequently in addition to hypomanic and depressive episodes. This patient does not demonstrate prominent mood symptoms (eg, elevated or depressed mood, sleep changes) and his illness course is constant rather than episodic. This patient most likely has schizophrenia.

Choice C: Delusional disorder is incorrect. Delusional disorder is characterized by the presence of one or more delusions for 1 month or longer without other psychotic symptoms. This patient demonstrates negative symptoms, and his delusions are impacting his social functioning, making schizophrenia the most likely diagnosis.

Choice D: Obsessive-compulsive disorder is incorrect. In obsessive-compulsive disorder, patients experience obsessions and perform compulsions to relieve the anxiety caused by their obsessions. However, delusions are typically more rigid, and the patient's affect is more likely to be anxious than blunted as negative symptoms are uncommon in obsessive-compulsive disorder. This patient demonstrates negative symptoms, and his delusions are impacting his social functioning, making schizophrenia the most likely diagnosis.

questions.retrievalTag: Antipsychotic use in elderly patients with dementia (Black Box Warning)
questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with progressive cognitive decline secondary to dementia, Alzheimer type, is brought to the physician by her son for a routine examination. The patient also has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She resides in a skilled nursing care facility. Two weeks ago, she was hospitalized for management of altered mental status and agitation secondary to a urinary tract infection. Nightly quetiapine therapy was begun for management of agitation at that time. She has not had recurrence of the agitation. Her other medications are donepezil, simvastatin, lisinopril, and hydrochlorothiazide. Her son says he is concerned about the ongoing use of quetiapine because his mother has been mildly unstable on her feet and more sleepy and lethargic since beginning the therapy. The patient's temperature is 37.4°C (99.3ºF), pulse is 89/min, and blood pressure is 135/84 mm Hg. Physical examination shows mild upper extremity rigidity and a shuffling gait. On mental status examination, she is mildly drowsy and has a pleasant demeanor; she is unable to provide a recent medical history. She is oriented to person but not to place. She recalls zero of three objects after 5 minutes, and she makes three errors with serial sevens.

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Continue quetiapine therapy and reassure the patient's son that this drug's benefits outweigh its risks

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Continue quetiapine therapy for 4 months and then taper the dose

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Discontinue quetiapine therapy and monitor the patient for behavioral changes

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Divide the current dose of quetiapine to morning and evening administration

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Switch from quetiapine to diazepam therapy

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Switch from quetiapine to risperidone therapy

questions.educationalObjective: The most appropriate next step is to discontinue quetiapine therapy and monitor this patient for behavioral changes. Antipsychotic medications, such as quetiapine, are associated with a black box warning of increased all-cause mortality in older patients with dementia and should be discontinued when no longer clinically indicated.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: Deprescribing unnecessary medication is often beneficial, especially when they cause adverse effects or in older patients with multiple comorbidities and polypharmacy, where medication interactions are a concern.
Antipsychotic medications carry a black box warning indicating an increased risk for all-cause mortality in older patients with dementia.
This increase in mortality is likely due to cardiac events (eg, heart failure) and infectious causes (eg, pneumonia).

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: When a patient is hospitalized and develops hyperactive delirium, antipsychotic medications are commonly added to assist with agitation.
If agitation secondary to delirium has improved by discharge, physicians should discontinue the antipsychotic medication prior to discharge while monitoring for worsening agitation.
However, sometimes medications may inadvertently remain on a patient's medication list after discharge, or the patient may still be experiencing residual agitation at discharge.
In this case, outpatient physicians should attempt to discontinue the antipsychotic medication as soon as it is no longer needed.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Some patients with dementia may have agitation associated with dementia.
In refractory cases that do not respond to other medications for agitation, the benefits of an antipsychotic medication (eg, maintaining patient and family safety) may outweigh the risks.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choices A, D, and F: Continue quetiapine therapy and reassure the patient's son that this drug's benefits outweigh its risks, divide the current dose of quetiapine to morning and evening administration, and switch from quetiapine to risperidone therapy are incorrect. This patient's agitation was secondary to delirium. Delirium and agitation typically improve with the treatment of the underlying condition, so long-term medications for agitation are typically unnecessary. Also, a morning dose of quetiapine is likely to increase daytime somnolence in this patient.

Choice B: Continue quetiapine therapy for 4 months and then taper the dose is incorrect. This patient's agitation was secondary to delirium. Delirium and agitation typically improve with the treatment of the underlying condition, so long-term medications for agitation are typically unnecessary. If patients have residual agitation after discharge, quetiapine may be temporarily continued. However, this patient's agitation was improved.

Choice E: Switch from quetiapine to diazepam therapy is not the most appropriate next step in management. Delirium and agitation typically improve with the treatment of the underlying condition, so long-term medications for agitation are typically unnecessary. Also, diazepam is on the American Geriatrics Society Beers Criteria of medications that identifies potentially inappropriate medications for older patients due to its risk for cognitive impairment, delirium, falls, motor vehicle collisions, and fractures.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old nursing student is admitted to the hospital because of fatigue, easy bruising, and blood in her urine. The nursing staff is suspicious when her oral temperature seems unusually high, and the temperature of her freshly voided urine is normal. When asked by the physician to discuss her symptoms, she signs out of the hospital.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient's presentation is inconsistent with a known medical condition and is suggestive of symptom fabrication (eg, artificially increasing her oral temperature and procuring a different person's urine for testing). In factitious disorder, patients consciously produce symptoms for primary gain.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: In factitious disorder, patients consciously produce symptoms (eg, purposely injure themselves, fake a fever by eating something warm, providing someone else's urine for laboratory study) for primary gain.
Diagnosis is supported by inconsistencies in medical history or physical examination and a history of extensive clinical evaluations.
If patients are directly confronted about their deceptive behaviors, it is common for them to discharge themselves from care.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Primary gain is the motivation to be cared for, which constitutes an unconscious motivator for the patient's conscious production of symptoms.

Early losses, neglect, or abuse may disrupt patients' identity and relationships, and adopting the sick role may represent patients' attempts to cope with stress and stabilize their identity and relationships.
Psychiatric comorbidities (eg, depression, substance use disorders, personality disorders) are common but not present in all patients.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause of factitious disorder is unknown, there is no universal prevention strategy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: To exclude a true fever and assist in establishing the factitious disorder diagnosis, a rectal temperature may be obtained in this patient.

Treatment includes regular follow-up with one physician who oversees management (as opposed to symptom-triggered appointments) and possible psychotherapy to address underlying coping difficulties that are driving the patients care-seeking behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: In patients with symptoms without clear structural causes but with no evidence of deceptive behaviors, somatic symptom disorder or functional disorders should be considered.
Malingering should be considered in patients who produce symptoms with a clear tangible gain (eg, disability benefits, avoiding prison time), which is known as secondary gain.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Conversion disorder is incorrect. Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) involves neurologic symptoms, such as sensory or motor dysfunction, that are not fully explained by objective findings on physical examination or imaging. This patient does not present with neurologic dysfunction. Additionally, volitional symptom production is inconsistent with functional neurological symptom disorder.

Choice C: Illness anxiety disorder (hypochondriasis) is incorrect. Patients with illness anxiety disorder demonstrate persistently excessive anxiety and preoccupation about the possibility that they may have or may acquire a serious illness such that they perform frequent health-related behaviors or exhibit maladaptive avoidance. This patient does not fear that she has a serious illness.

Choice D: Malingering is incorrect. Malingering is defined by falsification of symptoms to obtain a clear tangible gain (referred to as secondary gain). There is no clear tangible gain for this patient.

Choice E: Somatic symptom disorder is incorrect. Patients with somatic symptom disorder are preoccupied with a somatic symptom or multiple symptoms such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. However, patients with somatic symptom disorder do not volitionally produce their symptoms. In this case, there is evidence for symptom falsification.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine examination. She has hyperlipidemia well controlled with pravastatin and diet. She has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 10 years. She drinks six 12-oz beers on the weekends. She does not use illicit drugs. She works the night shift as a security guard for a condominium building and says she prefers not to interact with people. She is single and says she is reluctant to begin dating because she is not certain if she will be liked, and she does not want to feel rejected. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a constricted affect. She makes poor eye contact and speaks softly.
