It is most appropriate to obtain specific additional history regarding which of the following in order to confirm the diagnosis in this patient?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Difficulty making decisions without advice and reassurance from others

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Preoccupation with rules, lists, or organization

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Reluctance to engage in new activities because they may prove embarrassing

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: Tendency to appear indifferent to praise or criticism from others

questions.educationalObjective: This patient presents with pervasive social inhibition and rejection fears across contexts, which is most consistent with avoidant personality disorder. It is most appropriate to obtain specific additional history regarding this patient's reluctance to engage in new activities because they may prove embarrassing in order to confirm the diagnosis.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Avoidant personality disorder features the avoidance of interpersonal contact due to feelings of social inadequacy and hypersensitivity to rejection.
These individuals may present as extremely shy or timid, as seen in this patient.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood and are associated with significant social and occupational dysfunction.
Avoidant personality disorder is a cluster C (anxious or fearful) personality disorder.
It presents as pervasive social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, and hypersensitivity to negative evaluation (eg, rejection).

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause is unknown, there is no universal way to prevent avoidant personality disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Cognitive behavioral therapy may help address avoidance behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Social anxiety disorder presents similarly, but its behaviors may involve specific social situations as opposed to the pervasive inhibition characteristic of avoidant personality disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Difficulty making decisions without advice and reassurance from others is incorrect. These features are characteristic of dependent personality disorder, a cluster C personality disorder. This patient presents as timid but prefers avoiding people.

Choice B: Preoccupation with rules, lists, or organization is incorrect. This feature is consistent with obsessive-compulsive personality disorder, a cluster C personality disorder. This patient's perfectionism does not extend beyond social settings.

Choice D: Strange beliefs that influence behavior is incorrect. This feature is characteristic of schizotypal personality disorder, a cluster A (odd or eccentric) personality disorder manifesting with odd behavior and magical thinking and a constricted affect. Though this patient has a constricted affect because of social inhibition, she does not present with magical thinking or odd behavior.

Choice E: Tendency to appear indifferent to praise or criticism from others is incorrect. This feature is consistent with schizoid personality disorder, a cluster A personality disorder characterized by extreme social detachment and a cold, restricted affect. Patients with schizoid or avoidant personality disorders may prefer to work the night shift to avoid interaction with others, but patients with avoidant personality disorder would be more likely to do so out of fear of social rejection, as seen in this patient.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after being found unconscious by his wife. He responds diffusely to painful stimuli. His pulse is 48/min, respirations are 8/min with occasional 30-second episodes of apnea, and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. The pupils are sluggishly reactive to light. Deep tendon reflexes are intact. An overdose of which of the following is most likely responsible for these symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Barbiturate overdose presents with somnolence, obtundation, or coma, plus cardiorespiratory depression. This patient, unconscious with bradycardia, hypotension, bradypnea, and sluggish pupils, most likely has overdosed on a barbiturate.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Diagnosis of barbiturate overdose is based on clinical history and physical examination, as toxicologic screening may be delayed or inaccurate.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Barbiturates increase the central nervous system activity of y-aminobutyric acid, the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter.

Barbiturates are occasionally used as anticonvulsants in pediatric sedation and in the treatment of alcohol withdrawal, though their imprecise dosing, numerous medication interactions, and adverse effects of significant cardiorespiratory depression limit their use.
They are still used nonmedically, including in suicide attempts.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Avoidance of use is the mainstay of prevention of barbiturate overdose.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: There is no specific or appropriate treatment for barbiturate overdose;
supportive care to preserve life while permitting time for medication metabolism is the most appropriate management.
Patients with significantly decreased mental status or respiratory depression may require intubation for airway protection and mechanical ventilation.
Patients with cardiorespiratory depression may require intravenous fluid boluses or vasopressors.
Cardiac pacing is rarely indicated but may be required in severe or refractory cases.
Patients should also be tested and treated for co-ingestion, especially if overdose is in the context of attempted or suspected suicide.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient's obtundation plus cardiorespiratory depression narrows the differential diagnosis to overdose of a sedative.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A and Choice G: B-Adrenergic receptor antagonist and calcium-channel blocking agent are not most likely responsible for this patient's overdose. These medications can cause bradycardia and hypotension; however, they would not cause bradypnea/apnea and would not cause obtundation unless mental status was decreased due to severe hypotension, which is not the case with this patient.

Choice B: Amantadine is incorrect. Amantadine overdose has several features that are primarily neurologic and anticholinergic. Neurologic symptoms can include tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, disorientation, confusion, or delirium, and, in severe cases, seizures or myoclonus. Hallucinations and visual disturbances are also seen. Anticholinergic symptoms include tachycardia, hypertension, warm, flushed, dry skin, visual disturbances (eg, mydriasis), urinary retention, abdominal discomfort, and altered mental status/agitation.

Choice C: Amphetamine is incorrect. Amphetamines are stimulants that increase excitatory neurotransmitters. As such, elevated mood, loss of inhibition, hyperpyrexia, sympathetic excess, and increased thirst are common symptoms, along with tachycardia and hypertension. Severe intoxication can present with hyponatremia due to syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone along with increased consumption of water.

Choice D: Anticholinergic is incorrect. Anticholinergic overdose symptoms include tachycardia, hypertension, warm, flushed, dry skin, visual disturbances, urinary retention, abdominal discomfort, and altered mental status/agitation.

Choice E: Antihistamine is incorrect. Antihistamine intoxication presents with sedation/somnolence, though hemodynamic instability and apnea are not common features in isolated ingestion. Many antihistamines are also anticholinergic, which could also result in signs and symptoms such as tachycardia, hypertension, warm, flushed, dry skin, visual disturbances, urinary retention, abdominal discomfort, and altered mental status/agitation.

questions.retrievalTag: Sleep terror disorder features and differential
questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is brought to the office by her parents for an annual well-child examination. The parents report that the child has been well except for recent problems with sleep. The parents say that she awakens screaming about 2 hours after going to sleep about once weekly. They find her seated in her bed looking distressed and sweating. When they attempt to comfort her, she jumps out of bed or continues to scream. Soon afterwards, she improves and does not recall any of the events in the morning. Vital signs are within normal limits and physical examination shows no abnormalities.
questions.educationalObjective: The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is sleep terror disorder, which is characterized by episodes of sympathetic hyperactivity and vocalizations. Management involves reassurance.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Common in childhood, sleep terror disorder is characterized by episodes of sympathetic hyperactivity (eg, tachycardia, tachypnea, diaphoresis, mydriasis) and vocalizations (eg, screams) during non-REM (stage N3) sleep.
Episodes typically last minutes and occur during the first third of the night.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: During episodes, patients are typically partially awake and in a dissociative state, with features of sleep and wakefulness co-occurring on EEG and clinical assessment.
Patients are commonly amnestic to the episode.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause is unknown, there is no universal prevention strategy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Management involves reassurance, creating a safe sleep environment, and supporting sleep hygiene.
Many children outgrow sleep terror disorder by adolescence.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: In nightmare disorder, patients typically recall their episodes and experience nightmares during REM sleep (in 90-minute intervals throughout the night).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Benign rolandic epilepsy is incorrect. Benign rolandic epilepsy typically presents in childhood with unilateral sensorimotor symptoms of the face including drooling, twitching, and numbness. Sympathetic hyperarousal and vocalizations are atypical. Also, this patient does not present with focal facial sensorimotor symptoms.

Choice B: Nightmare disorder is incorrect. Nightmare disorder is a common parasomnia involving recurrent frightening or extremely dysphoric dreams leading to distress and disrupted sleep. In nightmare disorder, patients typically recall their episodes and experience nightmares during REM sleep (in 90-minute intervals throughout the night). This patient is amnestic to her episodes, and episode timing is more consistent with non-REM sleep.

Choice C: REM sleep behavior disorder is incorrect. REM sleep behavior disorder features a lack of normal muscle atonia during REM sleep, most commonly occurring in older male patients. Patients typically act out their dreams in a coordinated though sometimes violent manner. This patient experiences episodes during non-REM sleep and does not act out her dreams.
Choice E: Normal development is incorrect. This patient's symptoms are consistent with sleep terror disorder, which is considered a parasomnia.

questions.retrievalTag: Valproic acid hepatotoxicity symptoms and labs
questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with a history of bipolar disorder comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, poor appetite, and confusion. Three months ago, the patient was admitted for psychiatric hospitalization for a manic episode. Valproic acid was initiated at that time, and the patient's psychiatric symptoms improved. He returned to work as a real estate agent and says he had been doing well. He has hypertension and currently takes amlodipine and valproic acid. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 76 kg (168 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 142/82 mm Hg. He appears disheveled. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is oriented to person and place, but not time. He is able to recall one of three objects after 5 minutes. Results of which of the following laboratory studies are most likely to be abnormal in this patient?
questions.educationalObjective: This patient, presenting with nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, altered mental status, and disheveled appearance, is likely to have abnormal liver function tests and hyperammonemia caused by his valproic acid medication.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Valproic acid toxicity should be suspected in patients taking valproic acid who disclose signs of hyperammonemia (eg, psychomotor slowing, altered mental status, flapping hand tremor) and gastrointestinal symptoms (eg, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea). The diagnosis can be confirmed through measurement of serum valproic acid and ammonia concentrations, and liver function testing.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Valproic acid is an anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer used in the management of seizure disorders, bipolar disorder, and schizoaffective disorder. Valproic acid can cause adverse effects, such as gastrointestinal distress, sedation, tremor, weight gain, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and hepatic effects, that range from benign increases in transaminases to hepatotoxicity with hyperammonemia (as seen in this patient). Weight, complete blood count, and liver function should be monitored regularly in patients taking valproic acid.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Regular assessment for signs and symptoms of valproic acid toxicity along with monitoring of liver function, serum ammonia concentration, and complete blood counts can permit early diagnosis of toxicity along with discontinuation.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Discontinuation of valproic acid in the setting of toxicity is appropriate to allow clearance of the medication and its metabolites.
Dosing should be resumed at decreased concentrations, or a switch to an alternative agent may be more appropriate once toxicity resolves.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: The combination of gastrointestinal and neurologic symptoms in a patient taking valproic acid strongly suggests valproic acid toxicity, which is typically associated with abnormal liver function tests.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Leukocyte count is incorrect. Leukocyte count may be decreased in valproic acid toxicity; however, this patient's symptoms relate to gastrointestinal dysfunction and altered mental status, which implicates hepatic function more than lymphopenia consequently.

Choice C: Serum creatinine concentration is incorrect. Serum creatinine concentration is generally not affected by valproic acid. Most medications are metabolized through hepatic or renal routes; thus, increased serum creatinine concentration can indicate risk for toxicity. However, this patient's gastrointestinal and neurologic symptoms suggest hepatic dysfunction and not renal dysfunction as a cause.

Choice D: Serum glucose concentration is not the most likely laboratory study to be abnormal in this patient. Assessing glucose in a patient with altered mental status is a priority; however, hypoglycemia generally presents with abnormal vital signs such as tachycardia and with tremor or diaphoresis on examination. These findings are not seen in this patient.

Choice E: Urine toxicology screening is not the most likely laboratory study to be abnormal in this patient. Urine toxicology screening does not often correlate with current intoxication, as urine toxicology is generally positive long after intoxication has resolved.

questions.retrievalTag: Acute stress disorder timing and clinical features
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a follow-up visit 1 week after discharge from the hospital. Three weeks ago, he sustained second-degree burns to 14% of his total body surface area during a house fire. His family was in the house at the time but did not sustain injuries; the family dog died of smoke inhalation. The patient reports no notable pain and receives no medication. Since the fire, he has appeared preoccupied and has reported feeling "spaced out." His parents report that he refuses to talk about the fire and has been sleeping poorly, frequently awakening and crying out during the night. On questioning immediately after the episodes, the patient appears terrified and momentarily behaves as though he were in a fire but quickly calms down after he is reassured of his safety. Today, he is startled when the physician enters the examination room. The patient's temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 108/min, and blood pressure is 124/82 mm Hg. Physical examination shows healing burns over the upper torso; no other abnormalities are noted. On mental status examination, he says his mood is "sad" and he has a tearful affect. His speech is normal in rate and rhythm. His thought process is organized. He refuses to talk about the fire. There is no evidence of delusions or hallucinations.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient, recovering from an emotionally and physically traumatic event, is demonstrating symptoms of acute stress disorder, such as easy startling, mood changes, and intrusive thoughts.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Acute stress disorder presents with symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, avoidance, and changes in mood and/or thinking beginning within 3 days and lasting up to 1 month after a traumatic event.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: The inciting traumatic event is defined as involving actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violation.
Intrusive memories may manifest as daytime flashbacks, nightmares, or dissociative experiences.
Changes in arousal and reactivity may show as hypervigilance, easy startle, anxiety, restlessness, and/or decreased concentration.
Avoidance can present as refusal to discuss or think about the event.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: It is difficult to prevent acute stress disorder, as the incidence of traumatic events can be unpredictable.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Pharmacotherapy is typically not indicated, but supportive or cognitive behavioral therapy may be helpful.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Acute stress disorder occurs in the immediate short-term following a traumatic event, whereas post-traumatic stress disorder should be considered if symptoms begin or last over 1 month after the event.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B: Adjustment disorder is incorrect. Adjustment disorder refers to the onset of disproportionate distress within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and may be associated with anxiety, depression, and/or conduct disturbances. Though this patient has anxiety and depressive symptoms, he also has several other symptoms (eg, hyperarousal, nightmares) that are more consistent with acute stress disorder.

Choice C: Depersonalization-derealization disorder is incorrect. Depersonalization-derealization disorder is a dissociative disorder in which patients feel disconnected from their body and environment. Though the patient appears "spaced out," it is not clear whether he is feeling disconnected from his body and environment, and he has several other symptoms more consistent with acute stress disorder.

Choice D: Major depression is incorrect. Major depressive disorder includes 2 or more weeks of five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, psychomotor retardation, suicidal ideation, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (ie, decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance). Though this patient has a depressed mood, he does not demonstrate other major depressive disorder symptoms.

Choice E: Post-traumatic stress disorder is incorrect. Post-traumatic stress disorder refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts/memories of trauma, hyperarousal, avoidance of trauma reminders, and/or negative mood/thoughts resulting from a traumatic event. Symptoms last more than 1 month following the event or may have delayed expression, usually beginning within 3 months but may occur months or years later. This patient's symptoms began in the immediate short-term following a traumatic event and continue less than 1 month at the time of visit.

questions.stem:
A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police in a state of intoxication. They say they found him walking unsteadily, appearing confused and disoriented. He is unable to provide any history. Records show that he has been treated in the hospital for alcohol withdrawal six times during the past 2 years. On arrival, he is awake but confused and disheveled; he smells of alcohol. Physical examination shows horizontal nystagmus bilaterally. Muscle strength is 4/5 throughout the upper and lower extremities. There is no gait ataxia. Mental status examination shows a tangential thought process. Laboratory studies show:

[FIGURE: q25_fig1]
questions.educationalObjective: In a patient with alcohol use disorder, the presence of confusion and nystagmus suggests Wernicke encephalopathy, and intravenous vitamin B1 (thiamine) must be administered prior to dextrose to prevent worsening of neurologic dysfunction.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Wernicke encephalopathy presents with confusion, ataxia, and nystagmus in a patient with alcohol use disorder or other cause of thiamine deficiency.
The diagnosis is clinical but can be supported by decreased serum thiamine concentrations.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Wernicke encephalopathy is due to a deficiency of thiamine that typically occurs in patients with alcohol use disorder, although it can occur in other states of malnourishment.
While a treatable condition, administering dextrose before thiamine can worsen the deficiency, resulting in permanent symptoms in addition to confabulation (ie, Korsakoff syndrome).

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Abstinence from alcohol and maintenance of a balanced diet is the best prevention for Wernicke encephalopathy.
Patients with alcohol use disorder may also be given thiamine supplementation to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Thiamine is the best treatment for Wernicke encephalopathy.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient has severe hypoglycemia and should be administered intravenous 50% dextrose.
However, thiamine must be administered prior to dextrose if the patient is hypoglycemic.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Oral folic acid is incorrect. Deficiency of folic acid may be seen in individuals with alcohol use disorder and presents with macrocytic, megaloblastic anemia. Folic acid deficiency would not explain this patient's symptoms.

Choice B: Intramuscular vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) is incorrect. Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency presents with macrocytic, megaloblastic anemia, polyneuropathy, and cognitive difficulties. Cobalamin deficiency is more likely in individuals with pernicious anemia or who follow a strict vegan diet than those with alcohol use disorder.

Choice C: Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics is incorrect. Antibiotics would be correct in the management of acute infection; however, this patient does not show signs of infection, such as fever or leukocytosis.

Choice D: Intravenous 50% dextrose is incorrect. Dextrose given before thiamine in a patient with Wernicke encephalopathy can worsen neurologic symptoms. This patient needs intravenous dextrose; however, thiamine is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man comes to the office requesting medication for insomnia. He reports a 3-month history of right knee and abdominal pain, flatus, constipation, and diarrhea. He has an 8-year history of difficulty falling asleep, early awakening, excessive sleepiness, and depressed mood. He says that the last time he felt good was for the first few weeks after his wedding 5 years ago. During the past 4 years, he has felt inadequate at work and as a husband. He says, "I just drag my wife and everybody else down. I'm always screwing things up at work and home, and other people have to fix my mistakes." Abdominal examination shows distention and diffuse tenderness; bowel sounds are decreased. On mental status examination, he appears sad and frustrated. His speech is normal in rate but is monotonous. He is pessimistic about the physician's ability to help him. In addition to irritable bowel syndrome, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: This patient presents with longstanding depressed mood, decreased self-esteem, and insomnia, which is consistent with persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia).

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Persistent depressive disorder (PDD) refers to at least three depression symptoms that have endured for at least 2 years.
By definition, patients must report a depressed mood on more days than not.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Other symptoms of PDD include neurovegetative symptoms (ie, decreased energy, sleep disturbances, appetite disturbances), impaired concentration, low self-esteem, and hopelessness.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause is unknown, there is no universal prevention strategy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Emerging evidence indicates that PDD should be managed similarly to major depressive disorder (MDD).
Antidepressant medication plus psychotherapy is most effective. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are generally the first-line medications given their low adverse effect burden.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: PDD and MDD may present similarly. However, by definition, patients with PDD do not meet full criteria for MDD.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder is incorrect. Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder is a common cause of insomnia characterized by a shifted or irregular sleep-wake cycle relative to a typical day-night cycle. This patient experiences insomnia but does not report a shifted sleep schedule. Also, he demonstrates additional mood symptoms consistent with PDD.

Choice B: Dependent personality disorder is incorrect. Dependent personality disorder is a cluster C (anxious or fearful) personality disorder in which the patient is excessively submissive. This patient presents with decreased self-esteem but does not demonstrate a pervasive pattern of deferring to others' needs.

Choice C: Major depressive disorder is incorrect. Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of at least five of the following symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia (eg, decreased interest in socializing), guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms. Though this patient demonstrates a depressed mood and sleep difficulties, he does not meet the symptom number criteria for MDD.

Choice E: Somatic symptom disorder is incorrect. Patients with somatic symptom disorder are preoccupied with an existing somatic symptom such that these symptoms disrupt the patient's daily life. This patient presents with symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome but is not particularly preoccupied with these symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Altruism as a mature defense mechanism
questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman comes to the student health center for a health maintenance examination. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is in her second year of medical school and says she has been under a high level of stress because of studying for tests and increased time in clinical training. She recently broke up with her partner of 2 years and says the transition has been difficult. She has been focusing on a free clinic she started with her classmates that provides screening examinations for young mothers and their children. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows periorbital puffiness and infraorbital dark circles. On mental status examination, the patient describes her mood as "hanging in there," and she has a euthymic affect. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation. This patient is most likely using which of the following coping strategies?
questions.educationalObjective: This patient is most likely using altruism to cope with her current situation. Altruism helps a person manage intolerable emotions through the gratification of helping others.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: When any person experiences painful thoughts, realities, or feelings, such as anxiety or sadness, the mind has several unconscious methods of shielding the person from conscious awareness of the thought, reality, or affect. These methods are called defense mechanisms.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Altruism helps a person manage emotional conflict through helping others (eg, starting a free clinic).
Altruistic acts may relieve painful emotions by providing vicarious gratification or by helping individuals rewrite a past situation in which they did not receive the help they needed.
Altruism is considered an adaptive defense as it channels affects into socially helpful action.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Other adaptive defensive mechanisms include humor, sublimation, and suppression, among others.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B: Displacement is incorrect. In displacement, a person may transfer negative feelings from an unacceptable person to a more acceptable person. For instance, a woman cannot tolerate being angry at her spouse for cheating because of the fear she will need to divorce him, so her unconscious mind transfers the anger to her mother who she cannot divorce and is therefore a more acceptable target. This patient is not transferring negative feelings across people.

Choice C: Intellectualization is incorrect. In intellectualization, the individual avoids painful emotions by overreliance on abstract thinking rather than emoting. Though this patient is appropriately focusing on her medical school examinations, it does not appear she is excessively using abstract thinking.

Choice D: Projection is incorrect. Projection involves the patient attributing intolerable feelings to someone else. For example, a woman feels angry at her partner, but cannot tolerate feeling angry at her partner so perceives that her partner is actually angry with her. This patient does not present any evidence of projection.

Choice E: Rationalization is incorrect. In rationalization, individuals devise a reassuring explanation for their or others' actions. For example, the individual may avoid guilt related to a self-serving action by verbalizing a more socially acceptable motivation for an action (eg, "I only ate your slice of cake so that you will not gain weight"). This patient does not perform self-serving acts.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by police after recording violent threats on her former boss's voicemail. The patient states her former boss was using "remote control laser beams" and planting recording devices within her apartment in order to "monitor my every move" and prevent her from going to the police. The patient was fired from her job 6 months ago for unclear reasons. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She does not drink alcoholic beverages or use cannabis or illicit drugs. She appears thin, with long, uncombed hair and a blank expression. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, there is no evidence of hallucinations or suicidal ideation. During the interview, the patient becomes angry and says, "I'm not crazy, if that's what you think. It's my boss who you should be talking to; he's the crazy one." Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: This patient most likely has schizophrenia, which is characterized by at least 6 months exhibiting two of the following five symptoms: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms. Atypical antipsychotic therapy (eg, quetiapine) is the first-line treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Patients with schizophrenia demonstrate at least two of the following five symptoms: delusions (eg, persecutory delusions), hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia).
Negative symptoms may also manifest as poor personal hygiene. Many patients also illustrate prodromal periods of strange behavior and decreased functioning.
The total duration of symptoms must exceed 6 months to meet diagnostic criteria.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: The cause of schizophrenia is thought to include dopamine dysregulation, and brain imaging typically shows decreased cortical volume and enlarged ventricles.
A family history of bipolar disorder or schizophrenia is common in patients who are diagnosed.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Though there is no universal way to prevent schizophrenia, avoiding cannabis use during adolescence (especially in patients with a family history of serious mental illness) is likely beneficial for decreasing future schizophrenia risk.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Treatment of schizophrenia requires antipsychotics, which can be divided into two groups: typical (eg, haloperidol, fluphenazine, chlorpromazine) and atypical (eg, quetiapine, aripiprazole, olanzapine).

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Typical antipsychotics are effective. However, their potent dopamine receptor antagonism is associated with movement disorders, such as acute dystonia, parkinsonism, akathisia, and tardive dyskinesia.
As atypical antipsychotics carry a decreased movement disorder risk, they are commonly considered to be first-line treatments.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Clozapine is incorrect. Clozapine is an atypical antipsychotic medication used in treatment-refractory schizophrenia. Serious adverse effects, such as agranulocytosis, myocarditis, and seizures, prohibit the use of clozapine as an initial treatment for patients with schizophrenia.

Choice B: Divalproex is incorrect. Divalproex is an anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer used in the treatment of bipolar disorder and schizoaffective disorder. As it addresses mood but not psychotic symptoms, it is not a useful pharmacotherapy for schizophrenia.

Choice C: Lithium carbonate is incorrect. Lithium carbonate is a mood stabilizer used in the treatment of bipolar disorder and schizoaffective disorder. Lithium carbonate addresses mood but not psychotic symptoms.
Choice D: Lorazepam is incorrect. Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine medication used for acute anxiety, alcohol withdrawal, and seizures. Lorazepam does not address psychotic symptoms.
Choice E: Phenelzine is incorrect. Phenelzine is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor used in the treatment of depression and anxiety disorders. Phenelzine does not address psychotic symptoms.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man comes to the office because of a 4-week history of increasingly frequent episodes of anxiety, shortness of breath, sweating, tingling of his fingers, and a sensation of a racing heartbeat. The episodes now occur daily and last approximately 10 minutes before gradually resolving without treatment. He reports no apparent trigger for the episodes. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He does not drink alcoholic beverages or use illicit drugs. His pulse is 62/min, and blood pressure is 116/78 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is calm and describes his mood as "good." Cognition is intact. His serum thyroid-stimulating hormone and glucose concentrations are within the reference ranges. The most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient targets which of the following neurotransmitters?
questions.educationalObjective: Panic disorder is characterized by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks that are associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers. The most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient targets serotonin through the use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs).

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: This patient's monthlong history of increasingly frequent episodes of anxiety accompanied by shortness of breath, sweating, finger tingling, and a rapid heartbeat is most suggestive of panic attacks.
He most likely has panic disorder, which is characterized by recurrent panic attacks that are unexpected and associated with worry about future panic attacks or avoidance of panic attack triggers.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Panic attacks feature acute fear or anxiety that peak within minutes and are associated with four additional physical symptoms or associated mental states.

These additional symptoms may include heart palpitations or tachycardia, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, the sensation of choking, gastrointestinal distress, paresthesias, sweating, chills, trembling, derealization, the fear of dying, and/or the fear of losing control.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the cause of panic disorder is multifactorial (ie, involving various genetic and environmental triggers), there is no universal prevention strategy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Treatment typically includes psychotherapy and long-term SSRI (eg, sertraline) therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Short-term benzodiazepines can help with anxiety while awaiting the effect of SSRIs, which typically takes weeks.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Acetylcholine is incorrect. Medications that increase acetylcholine signaling (eg, cholinesterase inhibitors, such as donepezil) are used in dementia and myasthenia gravis. Acetylcholine antagonists (eg, benztropine) lead to relief of drug-induced parkinsonism along with sedation, urinary retention, and constipation. These medications are not used to treat anxiety disorders.

Choice B: Dopamine is incorrect. Dopamine agonists (eg, pramipexole) are used for symptomatic treatment in Parkinson disease, while dopamine antagonists (eg, antipsychotic medications) are used to treat schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. While antipsychotics can be used for refractory anxiety, SSRIs have stronger evidence and more benign adverse effect profiles, and are, therefore, first-line treatments for anxiety disorders.
Choice C: Glutamate is incorrect. Medications that antagonize glutamate (eg, memantine) are used in the treatment of dementia. These medications are not used to treat anxiety disorders.

Choice D: Histamine is incorrect. Hydroxyzine is an antihistamine medication used off-label as an as-needed anxiolytic. Hydroxyzine can be useful as an adjunctive medication for panic attacks, but SSRIs are evidence-based first-line treatments.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the office for a follow-up examination 6 weeks after uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery of a 3459-g (7-lb 10-oz) male newborn at term. She is exclusively breast-feeding. Her mother recently returned to her own home after spending the past 3 weeks helping the patient with the infant. The patient has no history of serious illness, and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She and her husband have not resumed sexual intercourse, and she has not initiated contraception. The infant sleeps for 3-hour intervals. The patient says she has difficulty falling asleep, even when the infant is sleeping. She says her husband is helpful but she has difficulty leaving the house and is overwhelmed by daily activities. She worries that she is not a good mother. She cries several times daily and has had loss of appetite. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination, including thyroid, breast, and pelvic examinations, shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is tearful and gives one-word answers. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: This patient most likely has major depressive disorder (MDD), peripartum onset. MDD presents with depressed mood, decreased interest, feelings of guilt or worthlessness, decreased concentration, neurovegetative changes (ie, changes in sleep and appetite, decreased energy, and slowed movement), and/or thoughts of death or suicide. MDD symptoms make daily activities (eg, leaving the house) challenging.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: A diagnosis of MDD is made when a patient experiences five or more of the following symptoms over a 2-week period or greater: depressed mood, anhedonia, change in appetite or weight, insomnia or hypersomnia, psychomotor agitation or retardation, fatigue, feelings of guilt or worthlessness, diminished ability to think or concentrate, and recurrent thoughts of death including suicidal ideation with or without a plan.
Patients commonly experience daily dysfunction because of their depressive symptoms.
This patient demonstrates several of these symptoms since the birth of her baby.
MDD with peripartum onset occurs during pregnancy or within 4 weeks after delivery.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: MDD with peripartum onset can lead to adverse consequences including a decreased likelihood of breast-feeding, impaired bonding with the infant, marital discord, poorer infant health, infanticide, and suicide.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Increasing psychosocial support, exercising, and seeking counseling may help prevent MDD with peripartum onset.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Management of MDD may involve psychotherapy (eg, cognitive behavioral therapy) and/or antidepressant therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Postpartum blues is a common, transient state that may be related to hormonal changes and resolves within 2 weeks of delivery.
Patients with postpartum blues do not meet MDD criteria.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B: Postpartum blues is incorrect. Postpartum blues is a common, transient state of sadness, crying, irritability, and/or anxiety that may be related to hormonal changes and resolves within 2 weeks of delivery. Patients with postpartum blues do not meet MDD criteria.

Choice C: Postpartum psychosis is incorrect. Postpartum psychosis refers to the postpartum onset of psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations). Though this patient has anxiety about being a good mother, she does not demonstrate a fixed, false belief that would constitute a delusion.

Choice D: Postpartum thyroiditis is incorrect. Though postpartum thyroiditis can present with anxiety, it also commonly presents with other somatic symptoms, such as heart palpitations and physical examination findings (eg, dry skin), along with laboratory study findings consistent with thyroid dysfunction.
Choice E: Normal postpartum course is incorrect. This patient demonstrates symptoms consistent with MDD, which is not typical in the normal postpartum course.

questions.retrievalTag: Paranoid personality disorder features and differential
questions.stem: A 50-year-old man comes to the office because of a 10-year history of anger outbursts. He says he did not want to come to this evaluation, but his wife begged him to come. He reports that he becomes angry a few times weekly when people attack his work or his character. At times, he has threatened people at work, but he has never hit another person. He explains that he has outbursts because others are taking advantage of him and do not appreciate his work. When asked about his marriage, he says he is satisfied with his wife but thinks she may be seeing another man though he has no evidence of infidelity. He says he has not had any changes in his mood. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He has no history of psychiatric treatment. The patient sits on the far end of the couch and appears guarded. He describes his mood as "fine" and has a tense and irritable affect. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation, or auditory hallucinations. His thought process is goal-directed.
questions.educationalObjective: This patient pervasively perceives that others are persecuting him, which is behavior consistent with paranoid personality disorder. Paranoid personality disorder presents with pervasive mistrust of others and viewing others as possessing malicious intent.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Patients with paranoid personality disorder view others as malevolent (such as this patient's distrust of his coworkers) and often question and distrust their motives as a result.
These individuals may present as angry and reluctant to rely on others.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Personality disorders are persistently maladaptive ways of relating to the self and to society that typically appear by early adulthood.
Personality disorders are associated with significant social and occupational dysfunction.
Paranoid personality disorder is a cluster A (odd or eccentric) personality disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause is unknown, there is no universal way to prevent paranoid personality disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Patients with paranoid personality disorder may be hesitant to engage in treatment, though some patients may benefit from psychotherapy targeting their paranoid ideation.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Schizotypal personality disorder along with psychotic disorders, such as schizophrenia and delusional disorder, should be excluded for this patient.
While all can present with paranoia, schizotypal personality disorder and schizophrenia both typically present with perceptual disturbances (eg, hallucinations), which are not seen in this patient.
Furthermore, patients with delusional disorder may have one or a few specific false beliefs, while patients with paranoid personality disorder are pervasively paranoid across all contexts.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Antisocial personality disorder is incorrect. Antisocial personality disorder is a cluster B (emotional or dramatic) personality disorder that arises from a deficit in empathy, resulting in pervasive violations of others' rights, aggression, and a hostile and manipulative attitude toward others. Though this patient experiences anger and hostility toward others, he does not violate others rights.

Choice B: Narcissistic personality disorder is incorrect. Narcissistic personality disorder is a cluster B personality disorder that is characterized by fragile self-esteem and compensatory arrogant, self-aggrandizing behavior to gain admiration, sometimes at others' expense. This patient's main issue is that he misinterprets others' intentions as being malicious toward him or unfairly criticizing him. His behavior is defensive rather than self-aggrandizing.

Choice C: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder is incorrect. Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder, a cluster C (anxious or fearful) personality disorder, features preoccupation with rules, lists, or organization. Though this patient speaks of his work, he is mainly preoccupied with perceived slights from others, consistent with paranoid personality disorder.

Choice E: Schizotypal personality disorder is incorrect. Schizotypal personality disorder is a cluster A personality disorder manifesting with odd behavior and magical thinking and a constricted affect. This patient does not demonstrate magical thinking or odd behavior.

questions.retrievalTag: Secondary neuropsychiatric symptoms and brain imaging
questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with chronic hepatitis is brought to the physician by his family because he appears depressed, much less talkative, and not as mentally acute as he had been. He forgets to complete chores after he begins them. He says that he is not depressed but does see dead relatives and hear their voices. He takes a thyroid supplement for hypothyroidism. His pulse is 90/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 140/88 mm Hg. He has mild asymmetric hyperreflexia on the right; muscle strength is 3/5 on the right and 4/5 on the left. He has visuospatial difficulties and bumps into a wall on the left when asked to walk across the room. His affect is slightly constricted, but he has no suicidal or homicidal ideation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Measurement of serum urea nitrogen concentration

questions.educationalObjective: The presence of focal neurologic deficits suggest a structural neurologic cause of this patient's depressive and cognitive symptoms and should prompt an MRI of the brain. This patient has chronic hepatitis as a malignancy risk factor, so primary brain cancer should be considered in the differential diagnosis.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: Patients who present with the combination of neuropsychiatric symptoms (eg, depression, psychosis, cognitive impairment) and focal neurologic deficits require brain imaging.
Focal neurologic deficits commonly indicate that a lesion of a particular central nervous system area (eg, cancer, stroke, abscess) is present.
MRI of the brain is the most sensitive imaging modality for detecting structural lesions.
Older age onset of psychiatric symptoms without a clear stressor or trigger is supportive of a secondary cause rather than a primary psychiatric disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Patients with primary brain cancer have highly variable clinical presentations depending on the tumor size and location.
The most common symptoms are headache, focal neurologic deficits, seizures, and neuropsychiatric symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Other localized lesions, such as stroke, hematoma, and abscess, should be considered.
Major depressive disorder is less likely given the focal neurologic deficits and gross cognitive impairment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Neuropsychological testing is incorrect. Neuropsychological testing can be used when the cause of a patient's cognitive impairment is unclear. However, the presence of focal neurologic deficits suggest a structural lesion.

Choices B, C, and D: Measurement of serum ammonia concentration, measurement of serum urea nitrogen concentration, and thyroid function tests are incorrect. Though abnormalities in these laboratory studies can present with cognitive impairment and associated psychiatric symptoms, focal neurologic deficits would be atypical. Thyroid dysfunction would also present with other physical examination signs, such as hair and skin changes.

Choice E: EEG is incorrect. EEG helps to assess seizure activity as the cause of cognitive or psychiatric symptoms. This patient does not show episodic motor signs consistent with seizures. Though subclinical seizures can cause cognitive impairment without visible seizure activity, MRI of the brain should be pursued first, as the presence of focal neurologic deficits suggest a structural lesion.

Choice G: Lumbar puncture is incorrect. Lumbar puncture may be pursued to exclude central nervous system infections or autoimmune encephalitis as the cause of neuropsychiatric symptoms. However, MRI of the brain is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis, as the presence of focal neurologic deficits suggest a structural lesion.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband because of a 6-day history of difficulty sleeping and changes in mental status. Her husband says that last night at 4 AM, he found her baking brownies and talking incessantly. One week ago, she was diagnosed with major depressive disorder, and treatment with duloxetine was begun. She also has hypertension and osteoporosis. Her other medications are furosemide, a multivitamin, and a calcium supplement. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows mild kyphosis. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. On mental status examination, she has an elated mood and a frequent giddy laugh. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She says that a late night talk show host is her guardian angel. Serum studies show a thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration of 1.2 uU/mL, thyroxine (T4) concentration of 6.5 ug/dL, and vitamin B12 (cobalamin) concentration of 329 pg/mL (N=160–950). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features

questions.educationalObjective: This patient is likely experiencing acute mania with psychosis. Given that symptom timing correlated with duloxetine use, this patient most likely has medication-induced mood disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Antidepressant initiation or dose escalation can lead to medication-induced mania.

Acute mania typically manifests with an irritable or euphoric (and at times labile) mood, increased energy, a decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, rapid speech and thinking, irresponsibility, and/or grandiose thinking.
In severe cases, patients can experience psychosis, as in this case where the patient appears internally stimulated (eg, late-night baking while talking to herself).

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Antidepressant-induced mania is somewhat common in patients with underlying bipolar disorder and less common, but possible, in patients with unipolar major depressive disorder.
Tricyclic antidepressants carry the highest risk, followed by serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Carefully screening for bipolar disorder prior to initiating antidepressants can help mitigate the risk for medication-induced mania.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Management typically involves antidepressant discontinuation and mood stabilizer initiation.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Underlying bipolar disorder should be considered in patients who have experienced antidepressant-induced mania.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Major depressive disorder with psychotic features is incorrect. Major depressive disorder with psychotic features includes 2 or more weeks of depressive symptoms (eg, depressive symptoms, neurovegetative symptoms) and psychotic symptoms that occur concurrently. This patient demonstrates a delusional belief secondary to manic symptoms but does not currently demonstrate depressive symptoms.

Choice C: Schizoaffective disorder is incorrect. Schizoaffective disorder is characterized by psychotic symptoms (eg, delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, and/or negative symptoms) along with significant mood episodes (eg, major depressive, manic episodes). Patients must demonstrate isolated psychotic symptoms without mood symptoms for at least 2 weeks. This patient demonstrates manic symptoms in conjunction with psychotic symptoms, indicating that her psychotic symptoms are likely secondary to her mood disorder.

Choice D: Schizophrenia is incorrect. Schizophrenia disorder involves 6 months or longer of two of the following: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, or disorganized behavior (known as positive symptoms), or negative symptoms (eg, flat affect, apathy, alogia). This patient demonstrates manic symptoms in conjunction with psychotic symptoms, indicating that her psychotic symptoms are likely secondary to her mood disorder.

Choice E: Serotonin syndrome is incorrect. Serotonin syndrome, the result of serotonin toxicity, can lead to the triad of acute confusion, autonomic hyperactivity (ie, hyperthermia, tachycardia, hypertension), and neuromuscular excitability (ie, tremor, myoclonus, hyperreflexia). This patient is hemodynamically stable and has a normal physical examination.

questions.retrievalTag: Lithium toxicity risk factors and prevention
questions.stem:
A 29-year-old man comes to the office for a routine follow-up examination. He was diagnosed with bipolar disorder 1 year ago, which has been treated with lithium carbonate. He takes no other medications. He drinks one to two beers monthly but does not smoke cigarettes. Vital signs are within normal limits, and physical examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:

[FIGURE: q34_fig1]

Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient to prevent adverse effects?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Eating a diet high in complex carbohydrates and fiber

questions.educationalObjective: This patient is taking lithium carbonate, which has a narrow therapeutic index, so recommending staying well-hydrated in hot weather is the most appropriate recommendation to prevent adverse effects.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: Lithium carbonate is used as a mood stabilizer in bipolar disorder.

However, it is cationic and renally cleared, and dehydration or imbalances in electrolytes can result in toxicity, which can be severe and cause permanent neurologic deficits if not promptly diagnosed and managed.
Lithium carbonate crosses the blood-brain barrier and placenta, and is excreted in breast milk.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: At therapeutic doses, lithium carbonate can cause adverse effects, such as tremor, nystagmus, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and thyroid dysfunction.

Risk factors for lithium toxicity include intentional or inadvertent overuse, dehydration (including that resulting from diabetes insipidus), or any factor impairing renal clearance (eg, inadequate fluid intake during hot weather).
Additionally, lithium carbonate is freely filtered by the kidneys and reabsorbed in states of dehydration or diuretic use, which can increase serum concentration and toxicity risk.
In severe toxicity, patients may experience seizures, arrhythmias, and coma. Some neurologic manifestations may be irreversible in severe cases.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Recognizing lithium carbonate's adverse effects and toxicity risk factors highlights the importance of staying well-hydrated in hot weather to prevent increased serum concentrations.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Avoiding alcoholic beverages is not the most appropriate recommendation. While alcoholic beverages can cause dehydration through diuresis, this patient only drinks one to two alcoholic beverages monthly, which is not a significant amount and unlikely to influence his medications or hydration status in any meaningful manner.
Choice B: Avoiding international travel is incorrect. A patient with stable health status on stable long-term dosing of a medication does not need to be on any specific travel restriction.

Choice C: Eating a diet high in complex carbohydrates and fiber is incorrect. While eating such a diet may be appropriate depending on the patient's BMI and comorbidities and will likely be an excellent choice in disease prevention and management if he is otherwise healthy, such a diet will neither cause nor prevent lithium toxicity.

Choice D: Exercising regularly is incorrect. Exercising regularly is also an excellent preventive healthcare measure. Excessive exercise or exercise during hot weather could increase his risk for dehydration, so he should also be counseled to remain well-hydrated in these scenarios. However, exercising regularly will neither cause nor prevent lithium toxicity.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old man is brought to the office by his daughter because of progressive memory loss, decreased concentration, and difficulty sleeping during the past month. The patient says that he finds it difficult to recall such details as appointments, people's names, and his own whereabouts. He recently stopped volunteering at a local hospital because he was worried that he would appear "stupid" to his colleagues. His daughter states that the patient used to be a very active person but now spends most of his time in bed. He has a history of coronary artery disease and intermittent angina on exertion, for which he takes nifedipine and isosorbide dinitrate. He does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. He is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he appears fatigued and has a constricted affect. His speech is soft and often halting. On cognitive testing, he makes numerous errors with recall and calculation but can usually provide the correct answers after prompting. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's memory loss?
questions.educationalObjective: This patient, who presents with the subacute onset of memory difficulty along with several depressive symptoms (eg, affect constriction, feelings of worthlessness, decreased interest in activities, decreased energy, decreased concentration), is likely experiencing major depressive disorder (MDD).

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Symptoms of MDD include 2 or more weeks of depressed mood, anhedonia, guilt or worthlessness, difficulty concentrating, suicidal thoughts, and/or neurovegetative symptoms (ie, decreased energy, sleep disturbance, appetite disturbance).

Older patients may experience depression-related cognitive dysfunction (previously known as pseudodementia), which most commonly manifests as decreased processing speed, impaired attention, executive dysfunction, and memory impairment.
Decreased effort on cognitive testing is also supportive of this diagnosis.

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: Older patients may view their neurovegetative symptoms as a manifestation of normal aging or a comorbid medical condition rather than depression.
Consequently, older patients who are depressed may struggle to express their depressed mood.
MDD should be suspected in this patient with many other depressive symptoms.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: As the precise cause of MDD is unknown, there is no universal prevention strategy.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Mood and cognition typically improve with antidepressant medication and/or psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Acute and fluctuating cognitive changes are consistent with delirium.
Chronic, progressive cognitive changes (particularly when mood symptoms are absent) are more consistent with dementia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Delirium is incorrect. Delirium is an acute confusional state commonly associated with acute medical illness in older patients. Delirium presents with acute disturbances in awareness, attention, and baseline cognition that fluctuate in severity over the course of the day. This patient instead presents with subacute, nonfluctuating cognitive symptoms and does not present with a clear precipitating medical cause of delirium.

Choice B: Dementia, Alzheimer type, is incorrect. Dementia, Alzheimer type, causes chronic, progressive impairments in language, memory, perception, motor skills, attention, orientation, problem-solving, and executive function. A subacute course that involves associated depressive symptoms and decreased effort on cognitive examination is more consistent with MDD.

Choice C: Dissociative amnesia is incorrect. Dissociative amnesia refers to the inability to recall traumatic or anxiety-inducing events secondary to psychological distress. This patient instead has more generalized cognitive deficits, presenting with inattention, memory difficulty, and decreased effort on cognitive testing.

Choice E: Normal aging is incorrect. Though normal aging is associated with mild deficits in processing speed, attention, memory, and executive function, these deficits do not typically cause daily dysfunction, as seen in this patient. Also, this patient presents with depressive symptoms, which are not consistent with normal aging but with MDD.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with schizophrenia is admitted to the hospital because of a 6-hour history of fever, sweating, and confusion. His only medication is haloperidol. His temperature is 40°C (104°F), pulse is 96/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 160/90 mm Hg. There is generalized muscle rigidity. Mental status examination shows lethargy and confusion. Haloperidol therapy is discontinued, and supportive care with intravenous fluids is started. Which of the following medications would most likely put this patient at increased risk for recurrence of his symptoms?
questions.educationalObjective: Metoclopramide, a dopamine antagonist, would place this patient at increased risk for recurrent neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS).

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: NMS is a life-threatening condition associated with the antagonism of dopamine that presents with altered mental status, muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, and autonomic dysfunction (eg, tachycardia, sweating).

explanationSections.heading: Definition
body: NMS is commonly caused by antipsychotic medications (especially high-potency antipsychotic medications) and antidopaminergic nausea medications (eg, metoclopramide).
Rigidity results from dopamine antagonism in the nigrostriatal pathway, while autonomic dysfunction and hyperthermia may result from dopamine antagonism in the hypothalamus.

Characteristic laboratory abnormalities include leukocytosis and increased serum creatine kinase, lactate dehydrogenase, and alkaline phosphatase (due to prolonged muscle rigidity and consequent myocyte damage).
Electrolyte abnormalities (eg, hypo- or hypernatremia, hypocalcemia) may also be shown.

explanationSections.heading: Prevention
body: Avoidance of antidopaminergic medications is the only specific prevention, though such avoidance is not always feasible especially if the benefits of an antidopaminergic medication outweigh the risks.
Patients for whom therapy with antidopaminergic agents has failed due to NMS should be switched to alternative therapeutic agents to prevent recurrence.

explanationSections.heading: Treatment
body: Treatment is supportive and includes discontinuation of the offending medication, hydration, cooling blankets, and benzodiazepines for agitation if needed.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient's acute hyperthermia, altered mental status, muscle rigidity, and autonomic instability are consistent with NMS.
Understanding that NMS is caused by antidopaminergic agents narrows the differential of safe pharmacologic agents to those other than metoclopramide or other dopamine antagonists.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: Bromocriptine is incorrect. Bromocriptine, a dopamine D2 receptor agonist, is used in the management of hyperprolactinemia and acromegaly, among other indications. In NMS, dopamine agonists may help restore dopaminergic activity and counteract the dopamine receptor blockade caused by antipsychotic medications.
Choice B: Dantrolene is incorrect. Dantrolene is a skeletal muscle relaxer used in the management of malignant hyperthermia and refractory NMS. Its use is appropriate in this setting.

Choice C: Diphenhydramine is incorrect. Diphenhydramine is an antihistamine and anticholinergic used in the management of allergies, anxiety, and insomnia. Diphenhydramine would be contraindicated in anticholinergic syndrome. This patient presents with NMS.

Choice D: Lorazepam is incorrect. Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine that increases y-aminobutyric acid activity in the central nervous system. It may be used for sedation or to reduce muscle rigidity in the management of NMS, but it would not put this patient at increased risk for recurrence of his symptoms.

questions.retrievalTag: Communicating bad news and Down syndrome counseling
questions.stem: A 39-year-old primigravid woman at 18 weeks' gestation comes to the office with her husband for a follow-up examination to discuss the results of an amniocentesis performed 2 weeks ago. The patient and her husband are informed that the results of the amniocentesis indicated that the infant will have Down syndrome. After receiving this information, the patient and her husband are distraught and visibly upset. The patient is tearful and asks the physician, "What are we supposed to do now? I thought I was having a healthy baby." Which of the following is the most appropriate response by the physician at this time?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: "I am sorry that this is so difficult, but there are a number of support groups available for parents of children with Down syndrome."

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: "Let's begin with any questions you might have about the diagnosis so I can best help you during this difficult time."

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: "Several options are available for you to consider, including adoption or termination of the pregnancy."

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: "Since this is devastating news, let's sit quietly for a moment to think about your concerns."

answerChoices.label: E
answerChoices.text: "While raising a child with Down syndrome will present some challenges, you should be assured your child can live a healthy life."

questions.educationalObjective: Counseling parents of a fetus with a new diagnosis of a genetic disorder can be challenging. The first step is investigating what they already know about the disorder by asking if they have any questions.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: Down syndrome is a genetic disorder characterized by an array of morphologic features and intellectual disability.

Effective communication after the diagnosis of Down syndrome is disclosed is important and initially should focus on what the parents already know to help them understand the condition and make knowledgeable decisions regarding steps moving forward.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: When breaking the news of a life-changing diagnosis to a patient or their family members, the physician should first ask open-ended questions to maintain therapeutic alliance and understand how to best provide information to the patient or their family.
This information will provide the physician an opportunity to clarify misconceptions and ensure that the parents have information to make decisions moving forward.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: Depending on patient preference and state legal status, a number of options are available for them to consider;
however, the most important initial step is to elicit what they already know about Down syndrome.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice A: "I am sorry that this is so difficult, but there are a number of support groups available for parents of children with Down syndrome" is incorrect. This response does not help the parents understand the diagnosis and assumes they will continue with the pregnancy.

Choice C: "Several options are available for you to consider, including adoption or termination of the pregnancy" is incorrect. While options available to the parents will need to be explained, this response does not allow for an initial attempt to understand or clarify the parents' understanding of Down syndrome, so they can make a knowledgeable decision regarding steps moving forward.

Choice D: "Since this is devastating news, let's sit quietly for a moment to think about your concerns" is not the most appropriate response. It may be helpful to allow time for the parents to absorb new information and think about questions; however, it is not appropriate to label the diagnosis of Down syndrome as devastating, and this option avoids gathering information to best advise the parents.

Choice E: "While raising a child with Down syndrome will present some challenges, you should be assured your child can live a healthy life" is not the most appropriate response. It is true that people with Down syndrome can live healthy lives. However, this statement does not elicit the parents' understanding of Down syndrome and it assumes they will proceed with the pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of a recent change in her behavior. Her parents recently found an open pack of cigarettes in her jacket pocket and cannabis paraphernalia in her room. The patient reports that during the past month she has been smoking two cigarettes daily and has smoked cannabis with friends on three occasions. She also has consumed one beer on two occasions. She states, "It's no big deal, and I was planning to stop anyway." Her grades have dropped from a B+ to a B average, although she attends school regularly. She also has been spending more time with kids her parents are unsure about. Her sleep and appetite are normal. Her paternal grandfather has a history of alcohol use disorder. The patient is casually dressed, wearing baggy pants and a head scarf. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she appears sullen but says she is not depressed and does not have anxiety. Urine toxicology screening for illicit drugs is positive for cannabis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Counsel the patient and family about the risks of substance abuse

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: Recommend an inpatient substance abuse treatment program

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Recommend an outpatient substance abuse treatment program

questions.educationalObjective: This patient's presentation, which includes rebellious and exploratory behavior, substance use that is not yet impairing her academic or familial life, and attitude change represent normal teenage behavior and exploration. She and her family should be counseled about the risks of substance use, as while she is maintaining her grades and family life for now, progressive or increasing substance use may eventually impair one or more aspects of her life.

explanationSections.heading: Key Concept
body: During the teenage years, patients may begin to rebel against parental or scholastic authority, explore and try things without complete consideration of consequences, and express their desires and wishes as they transition from childhood to adulthood. In this case, trying cannabis, cigarettes, and alcohol in limited fashion is generally normal exploration, though parents and physicians have a duty to guide and advise patients about safe choices to prevent negative consequences.

explanationSections.heading: Explanation
body: Cannabis is a hallucinogen that causes euphoria, anxiety, paranoia, perception of slowed time, impaired judgement, increased appetite, dry mouth, slow speech, prolonged response latency, lassitude, and inability to perform complex tasks, though severity of cannabis intoxication is based on the amount used and patient tolerance.
Her grades, while slightly lower, are above average. She continues to care about herself.

These features argue for a teenager trying and exploring aspects of life and learning her decision making is undeveloped in teenagers. As such, parents and physician have a duty to counsel and guide about safe choices, and to prevent negative consequences, such as traumatic accidents, addiction, failing grades, undesired pregnancy, and loss of relationships.

explanationSections.heading: Differential
body: This patient's limited cannabis use and absence of substantial life-affecting consequences indicates that counseling is all that is required currently. If her substance use were more severe or if it were affecting aspects of her life, substance use disorder treatment programs might then be necessary.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answers
body: Choice B and Choice C: Recommend an inpatient substance abuse treatment program and recommend an outpatient substance abuse treatment program are not the most appropriate next steps in management. This patient's substance use is on an inconsistent basis and is not currently limiting or significantly affecting her life or the lives of others.

Choice D: Begin nicotine patch therapy is incorrect. This patient has been smoking two cigarettes daily, which is likely not significant enough to merit nicotine replacement therapy. Furthermore, she states she is planning to stop using substances, which at her level of use, may be enough to maintain cessation. If she is unsuccessful or if she begins smoking more heavily and wishes to quit, nicotine replacement therapy could be considered.

Choice E: Begin a trial of bupropion is not the most appropriate next step in management. Bupropion is used as an antidepressant and smoking cessation aid. This patient does not have clinical signs of major depressive disorder, and her smoking is at a minimal level.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man with alcohol use disorder is brought to the emergency department by his family 4 hours after ingesting 10 to 12 acetaminophen tablets. Over the past 7 years, he has had withdrawal seizures and multiple admissions for detoxification. He is alert and oriented and is slightly tremulous. His pulse is 92/min and regular. The liver is palpated 1 cm below the right costal margin and has a span of 13 cm. Insertion of a nasogastric tube yields clear fluid. Activated charcoal is administered. His serum acetaminophen concentration is 180 ug/mL (therapeutic range=10-30). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answerChoices.label: A
answerChoices.text: Discharge from emergency department with psychiatric follow-up

answerChoices.label: B
answerChoices.text: 24-Hour observation followed by repeat measurement of serum acetaminophen concentration

answerChoices.label: F
answerChoices.text: Admission to the psychiatric unit for evaluation of suicidal ideation

questions.educationalObjective: An acetaminophen concentration of 180 ug/mL at 4 hours postingestion is a hepatotoxic concentration and an indication for therapy with oral administration of N-acetylcysteine. In this case, this patient likely attempted suicide via acetaminophen overdose.

explanationSections.heading: Evaluation
body: Acetaminophen poisoning presents with nonspecific symptoms of fatigue, malaise, and abdominal pain followed by signs and symptoms of liver failure, including jaundice, altered mental status, coagulopathy, and marked increases in serum transaminase concentrations.
